Scholars are mostly still okay using the term Reconquista or little-r reconquista for a couple of reasons. First, the map of Iberia did eventually shift from predominantly Islamic with a bit of Christian territory in the north to zealously Latin Christian, *if* we are using those lovely textbook color-coded political maps. Second, the papacy *did* attempt to make the Christian kingdoms' advance a sort of 'crusade in the West', offering soldiers the same indulgence (time off purgatory) for participation in Iberians wars as in the Near East *and* striving to redirect western French and Spanish soldiers from Jerusalem to Iberia. 
But as Robin Vose has remarked, "reconquista" is somewhat of an anachronism, more convenient than historically accurate. The political situation in Iberia, especially during the *fitna* (era of the taifa kingdoms), and the peninsula's pattern of settlement make the situation on the ground much messier.
The taifas emerge as *rivals* out of the breakdown of the Cordoba caliphate, bickering/warring with each other as they jockeyed for power. Simply in terms of territorial possession, why wouldn't a Muslim king choose to ally with a Christian lord against a Muslim king who threatened his territory more immediately than the Christian? He might even gain some land in the process! The "reconquista" was not unidirectional--in fact, control of land along the frontier flowed back and forth quite a bit, although the *net* result was gain for various Christian lords.
The second thing to keep in mind is the spare settlement of the Iberian interior. Most of the "Christian"-"Muslim" frontier was actually unoccupied land or pastoral grazing--the colored blobs on a political map don't necessarily represent day-to-day power or even residence. When Christian lords conquered new territory, for example, they had to work *really* hard (i.e. pay people) to live there. In fact, Christian Iberia is one of the very few places in Latin Europe were Jews were allowed to own land for a period of time, since the new lords wanted basically any farmers they could get.
The taifa kingdoms, ultimately, would lose land but as a whole not fall to the Christians but to other Muslims! First the Almoravid and then the Almohad dynasties, who were more vehemently Muslim in the sense that they mandated stricter observance for Muslims (and in the case of the Almohads, occasionally massacred non-Muslims). Even in the Almohad army, however, we know there were a significant number of Christian mercenaries.
When you think about the reconquista as more a "net effect" that the papacy attempted to shape, but driven forward by individuals concerned most of all with their own power and authority, the complexity of the situation far beyond simply "Christian versus Muslim" pops into place.

The paper titled "Intelligible Language Modeling with Input Switched Affine Networks" is a well-structured and comprehensive study that provides significant insights into the field of language modeling. The authors have done an excellent job in presenting a novel approach to language modeling using input switched affine networks. The introduction of the paper is well-written, providing a clear overview of the topic and the motivation behind the study. The literature review is thorough and provides a solid foundation for the research. The authors have done a commendable job in identifying the gaps in the existing literature and positioning their research within this context. The methodology section is detailed and provides a clear explanation of the input switched affine networks. The authors have also provided a clear explanation of how this new approach improves upon existing methods. The use of diagrams and illustrations to explain the methodology is particularly helpful. The results and discussion sections are well-presented and insightful. The authors have done a good job in interpreting the results and discussing their implications. The use of tables and graphs to present the results enhances the readability of the paper. However, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the limitations of the study and potential areas for future research. Additionally, while the technical language used in the paper is appropriate for the subject matter, a glossary of terms could be helpful for readers who are not familiar with the field. In conclusion, this paper is a valuable contribution to the field of language modeling. The authors have presented a novel approach to language modeling that has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of language processing tasks. The paper is well-written, well-structured, and provides a comprehensive overview of the topic.

The Essential Kenny Loggins is the third compilation and second greatest hits album by American singer-songwriter Kenny Loggins, released on November 19, 2002. It is part of Sony BMG's Essential series of compilation albums and includes tracks from Loggins' solo output, as well as tracks from his Loggins and Messina days. A limited edition was released with seven additional tracks. Kenny Loggins – vocals, rhythm guitar, harmonica, acoustic guitar Jim Messina – vocals, lead guitar, electric mandolin, acoustic guitar (tracks 1-7) Stevie Nicks – lead and backing vocals on "Whenever I Call You Friend" Merel Bregante – backing vocals, drums (tracks 1-7) Lester "Al" Garth - violin, recorder, alto and tenor saxophones (tracks 1-7) Jon Clarke – flute, oboe, recorder, baritone saxophone, soprano saxophone, tenor saxophone (tracks 1-7)

Becoming a fan of the "Where in the World is Carmen Sandiego" TV game show involves understanding the show's format, characters, and unique elements. Each episode began with an introduction of the show's premise, followed by the introduction of the contestants, referred to as 'gumshoes'. The game was played in several rounds, with the gumshoes answering questions to earn points and advance in the game. The first round involved a series of questions, with the gumshoes signaling their answers by pressing a buzzer. The second round was more interactive, with the gumshoe pulling on a handle that came down from the ceiling to 'put the Crook away'. The show's theme music played a significant role, especially when the gumshoe would write down where they'd like to go if they won the grand prize. This was often accompanied by a phone tap between the Crook and the gumshoe, adding to the suspense of the game. The bonus round, known as 'The Map round', was a crucial part of the game. The outcome of this round determined whether Carmen Sandiego escaped or was captured, as revealed in the newspaper sequence that followed. The show was hosted by Greg Lee, with the help of the house band, Rockapella. The villains on the show, including the fashion queen Contessa, added an extra layer of intrigue and excitement. The set of the show was designed to resemble a detective agency, with the gumshoes dressed in trench coats. The show aired on PBS and was known for its educational content and entertaining format. The announcer for the show was the voice actor, Scottie Haskell. The show also had a spin-off, "Where in Time is Carmen Sandiego", which followed a similar format but with a focus on history. Becoming a fan of the show involves not only watching the episodes but also understanding the unique elements that made it a hit. From the oddball episodes to the memorable villains, "Where in the World is Carmen Sandiego" offers a unique blend of education and entertainment.

Soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR), also known as receptor-bound iron, or iron-binding capacity, is a protein that in humans is encoded by the STFR gene. sTfR binds ferric iron but does not transport it. Soluble transferrin receptor plays a critical role in iron metabolism by monitoring the body's iron stores. When iron levels are low, as in the case of iron deficiency anemia, the liver increases production of soluble transferrin receptor to attract iron from degraded red blood cells. When iron levels are high, as in the case of elevated iron stores or blood loss, the body reduces production of soluble transferrin receptor to prevent acquiring too much iron.[1] Iron is essential to the life of a cell. Iron enters the cell through transferrin, which transports iron through the bloodstream. The amount of iron in the body is regulated by production of the soluble transferrin receptor, which binds iron when the body's iron levels are low, and absorbs iron into transferrin when iron levels are high. The soluble transferrin receptor plays a critical role in iron metabolism by monitoring the body's iron stores. When iron levels are low, as in the case of iron deficiency anemia, the liver increases production of the soluble transferrin receptor to attract iron from degraded red blood cells. When iron levels are high, as in the case of elevated iron stores or blood loss, the body reduces production of the soluble transferrin receptor to prevent acquiring too much iron.[1] Iron is essential for the function of hemoglobin, which carries oxygen in red blood cells, and myoglobulin, which helps oxygen enter muscle cells. Insufficient iron leads to the production of improperly shaped red blood cells, which can result in anemia. Excess iron can cause iron overload, which can result in liver and heart failure.

"Stealing First Base" is the sixteenth episode of the twenty-first season of the American animated television series The Simpsons. It originally aired on the Fox network in the United States on March 21, 2010. In this episode, Bart falls in love with a girl named Nikki from a second fourth-grade class, but when he kisses her, Nikki begins treating Bart like dirt. Meanwhile, First Lady Michelle Obama teaches Lisa that there is no shame in being an overachiever, and Nelson Muntz teaches a blind boy how to be a schoolyard bully. The episode was written by John Frink and directed by Steven Dean Moore. The episode was also watched by 5.69 million households and received a 2.8/8 in the 18-49 Nielsen Rating. The episode received mixed reviews from critics. The episode guest stars Sarah Silverman as Nikki and Angela Bassett as Michelle Obama. Bart's fourth-grade class is merged with another fourth-grade class when teacher Mrs. Krabappel is absent. In the crowded classroom, Bart is forced to sit by a new student named Nikki. At first, they dislike each other, until Nikki admires Bart's artistic skills. Bart seeks romance advice from Homer, who passes him off to Grandpa Abe. After Grandpa advises Bart to kiss Nikki, however, when Bart does so after the two skateboard together, she recoils in disgust. Nikki's attorney's parents threaten to sue the school unless it is declared an "affection-free environment". Superintendent Chalmers causes a play in which Willie plays Nikki and Skinner plays Bart who was strangled by Homer after calling him a “Fatso”, meaning they are forced to kiss. Bart is confused at the outcome of this seemingly innocent action, and his confusion is later amplified when Nikki hides in his locker and kisses him again. Meanwhile, Lisa becomes popular when she receives an F on a test, but becomes unpopular again when it is revealed that her test was mistakenly given to Ralph, as both tests were mixed up as the F grade was supposed to be given to Ralph who had written Lisa's name on his test. Angry about being ostracized for being an overachiever once again, Lisa blogs about it, and her post is noted by a mysterious blogger known as Flotus 1 who turns out to be First Lady Michelle Obama. Obama drops by Springfield Elementary to give a speech about the importance of academics and recommends that the students should be nice to Lisa and other overachievers like her friends, Martin Prince and Allison Taylor. Bart and Nikki have been watching this speech from the roof, and Bart confesses to Nikki he does not understand her ever-changing moods. They argue and Bart stumbles, falling off the roof. Nikki says "I love you," but seeing that Bart is breathing treats him badly again. Bart stops breathing again, but the school's "no-touch" policy prevents anyone from performing CPR. Nikki defies the policy and revives Bart via mouth-to-mouth, thus setting off a montage of kiss scenes from various movies, some of which (such as Alien 3) never even had kiss scenes in them. When Bart awakes, Nikki's mood changes yet again and Bart remains in a state of complete confusion over female behavior. He tells her he does not care what happens between her and him, but then changes his mind and yells out "I love you!" after she kisses him once again. Throughout the episode, Nelson Muntz has befriended a blind boy and teaches him how to laugh at the misfortunes of others. In the end, the blind boy tricks Nelson into believing that a punch he sustained after insulting Nelson restored his sight. When he reveals to Nelson that this was a prank and laughs at Nelson's signature "haw haw", Nelson is as impressed as he is touched by the achievement. The episode is written by John Frink and directed by Steven Dean Moore, his second director credit for the season after "O Brother, Where Bart Thou?". The episode features guest star Sarah Silverman as Nikki (Bart's crush) and Angela Bassett as Michelle Obama. In September 2016, executive producer James L. Brooks told Variety that the crew had been "aggressive" in trying to get Obama to voice herself, and received a rejection note saying "good try". In the classroom, Nikki shows Bart a book about vampires which looks like the Twilight series book New Moon, which reads Red Moon. In the scene where Nikki performs CPR on Bart, recreations of kissing scenes from a variety of films and television shows are shown. These scenes are (in order); From Here to Eternity (1953), Gone with the Wind (1939), The Quiet Man (1952), Lady and the Tramp (1955), Planet of the Apes (1968), On Golden Pond (1981), Ghost (1990), Spider-Man (2002), Star Trek (2009), Beauty and the Beast (1987–1990), The Public Enemy (1931), WALL-E (2008), Alien 3 (1992), The Godfather: Part II (1974), and an All in the Family (1971–1979) episode in which Sammy Davis Jr. guest starred. The sequence, and the accompanying musical score, are a nod to Cinema Paradiso (1988), directed by Giuseppe Tornatore, with score by Ennio and Andrea Morricone. The Itchy & Scratchy cartoon is a parody of the 1982 film Koyaanisqatsi: Life Out of Balance, here titled Koyaanis-Scratchy: Death Out of Balance. In its original American broadcast, "Stealing First Base" was watched by 5.69 million households getting a 2.8/8 in the 18-49 Nielsen Rating. The episode came in second in its time slot and was the second most-watched show on Fox that night, after a new episode of Family Guy. Emily VanDerWerff of The A.V. Club criticized the episode, giving it a C+, calling the harassment storyline "pointless and stupid" although praised the Nelson subplot. However, Jason Hughes of HuffPostTV praised the storyline, writing "I applaud the series for facing such an issue with such intensity, and handling it with humor and compassion". Robert Canning of IGN gave the episode an 8 out of 10, saying that "Nikki turned out to be a fun and memorable character." Overall he stated that "Although ‘Stealing First Base’ fed us some old ideas, it did so with a lot of great bits...and some very strong guest voices." The scene where Bart and Nikki were performing a skateboard parkour contains the song "Chase" by Giorgio Moroder on the original FOX broadcast. When the episode is shown in syndication, "Chase" is replaced by a musically similar composition. The classical music that is heard during the montage of movie kiss scenes is currently believed to be orchestrated specifically for the show, rather than being a score that was written independently and used during the program. Original music is from Cinema Paradiso - "Love Theme", composed by Andrea Morricone.

Gau Island is an island of the New Georgia Islands in the South Pacific Ocean, within the sovereign country of Papua New Guinea. Gau is one of the New Georgia Islands in the South Pacific Ocean. It is part of the Solomon Islands, although it is not one of the main islands in the Solomons group, but is located near the main island of New Georgia. It lies about 40 km (25 mi) northeast of New Georgia and is connected to it by a short stretch of water. It is also about 20 km (12 mi) northwest of the island of Rendova. Gau is a small island, only about 5 km (3.1 mi) long and 1.5 km (0.93 mi) at its widest. It has a small population of about 100 people. The island is covered in tropical rainforest and has a mountainous interior. The highest point is Mount Maravai, which is 1,530 m (5,010 ft) high. The island is home to a variety of bird species, including the gerygone, the silktail, the New Georgia honeyeater, and the Woodford's rail. Gau was the site of a significant battle in World War II. In January 1943, United States forces invaded the island as part of their campaign to capture the Solomon Islands. The invasion force, under the command of Rear Admiral Daniel E. Barbey, included the 1st Raider Battalion and the 2nd Battalion, 24th Marine Regiment. The Raiders were under the overall command of Lieutenant Colonel Edson D. Raff, while the 2/24 was led by Lieutenant Colonel Walter Asbury Hardee. The U.S. forces were opposed by a Japanese force of about 2,000 men, under the command of Lieutenant General Masao Maruyama. The battle for Gau began on the morning of January 20, 1943, when the Raiders landed on the island. The Marines were scheduled to land soon after, but due to a miscommunication, they did not land until the afternoon. This delay gave the Japanese time to reinforce their positions on the island. The Raiders and the Marines began their attack on the island from the west, while the Japanese defenders attacked from the east. The fighting was intense, with heavy casualties on both sides. The Americans were able to push the Japanese back, and by the end of the day, they had secured most of the island. The next day, the Marines landed on the island, and the two forces began a coordinated attack on the Japanese positions. The fighting continued for several days, until the Japanese forces were finally defeated and forced to withdraw from the island. The battle for Gau was a significant victory for the Americans, as it provided a base for further operations in the Solomons. It also boosted the morale of the American forces, who had been fighting in the Pacific for several months. The Raiders and the Marines suffered heavy casualties in the battle, with about 50 men killed and another 200 wounded. The Japanese forces also suffered heavy casualties, with about 100 men killed and another 200 wounded.

Problem with the Spartans was that while they had some of the best trained troops in Greece, their society as a whole was pretty much stagnant. 
First, very few of the people in Sparta were actually 'proper' Spartans, the vast majority were slaves called Helots, these slaves were a constant threat due to the possibility of revolt, and the fact that the Spartans themselves were pretty much forbidden to do any farming, crafting, or anything other than training for war, meant Helot rebellions were much more damaging than in other city states where citizens were at least somewhat sustainable. This system where the Spartans were full time soldiers and almost all the economic production was left to a slave force that was always in danger of revolting meant that Sparta had to spend a lot of their military just keeping the Helots in line.
Sparta also had big problems with a forever decreasing population, their society wasn't really based on the family unit like most of the other city states (like Athens), but to the state. Males were taken from their families at a young age and lived in a barracks separate from even their wives until about 30, at least. This, as you can imagine did not really help with baby-making T_T. Even if a baby were born there was a chance the elders might decide the baby was unfit and have the child left to die of exposure.
So pretty much: Sparta had very good soldiers because they could train to be soldiers full time, but the way their society was constructed meant that they had to spend most of their military power keeping the helots under control so they could have the food and supplies needed to maintain their full time soldiers. In addition, they also had a very low birth rate, meaning Spartan soldiers were very slow to replace.
Athens didn't need to waste so much manpower keeping their slaves in line, and their birth rate was much higher(because they didn't do that thing where husbands lived apart from their wives) meaning they could absorb losses much better than the Spartans.
The Spartans were a society that was pretty much incapable of growing, all their time, effort, and resources were spent just keeping their society running.

We propose an excitable convolutional neural network (CNN) model for solving the 2D path planning problem in this work. The proposed method is based on the concept that the output of each layer can be considered as a potential field, and the final solution will emerge when all layers are combined together. We show how to train such a multi-layered CNN using backpropagation through time with gradient clipping. In addition, we also present two different methods to combine multiple fields into one single field by applying either linear or nonlinear combination functions. Finally, we demonstrate our approach on several benchmark problems including maze navigation, robotics motion planning, and autonomous driving. Keywords: Convolutional Neural Network, Backpropagation Through Time, Gradient Clipping, Maze Navigation, Motion Planning, Autonomous Driving. 1 Introduction Convolutional neural networks have been widely used in computer vision applications [1]. Recently, they were applied to solve various types of optimization problems [2], which include image classification [3], object detection [4], semantic segmentation [5], etc.. However, most existing works focus only on optimizing a single objective function [6] - [8].In many real-world applications, there may exist more than one objective function [9]. For example, in robotic motion planning [10], it usually requires finding collision-free paths while minimizing energy consumption [11] ; in autonomous driving [12], it needs to find safe trajectories under both kinematic constraints [13] and dynamic traffic conditions [14] at the same time; in medical diagnosis [15], it should consider not only disease prediction [16] but also treatment recommendation [17] simultaneously; in computational biology [18], it has to optimize protein folding [19] and drug design [20] at the same time. Therefore, it becomes necessary to develop new algorithms to handle multi-objective optimization problems [21].Recently, deep reinforcement learning [22] was introduced to address multiobjective optimization problems [23]. It learns policies directly from raw data without requiring hand-crafted features [24]. However, its performance heavily relies on the quality of training data [25]. The paper titled "The Variational Walkback Algorithm" presents an innovative approach to training undirected graphical models with latent variables, specifically Boltzmann machines. The authors propose a new learning procedure that addresses the challenge of sampling from Monte-Carlo Markov chains, which may not mix well in the inner loop of training for each example. The authors' idea of locally carving the energy function everywhere so that its gradient points in the "right" direction is intriguing and well-presented. The paper builds on previous work on contrastive divergence, denoising autoencoders, generative stochastic networks, and unsupervised learning using non-equilibrium dynamics, which adds credibility to the proposed method. The proposed variational bound on the marginal log-likelihood of the data is a novel contribution to the field. The authors' explanation of the learning procedure, which involves walking away from data points and then training the operator to walk backwards, is clear and well-articulated. The gradual increase in temperature as we walk away from the data is a unique aspect of this algorithm, and the authors have done a commendable job of explaining how this provides a gradient on the parameters to maximize the probability that the transition operator returns to its previous state. The variant of the algorithm where there is no explicit energy function is also an interesting aspect of this work. The authors argue that this eliminates the need for symmetric weights, which makes the models more biologically plausible. This is a significant point that could have far-reaching implications in the field. However, the paper could benefit from more detailed explanations of the mathematical derivations and the algorithm's implementation. Additionally, the authors should consider providing empirical evidence to support their claims. This could include experiments comparing the proposed method with existing methods, as well as demonstrating the algorithm's performance on real-world datasets. Overall, the paper is well-written and presents a novel approach to a recognized problem in the field. With some improvements, it could make a significant contribution to the literature on machine learning and graphical models.

Centre Sèvres is a prominent Jesuit higher education institution located in Paris, France. It is renowned for its comprehensive programs in philosophy and theology. The institution is named after its location on Rue de Sèvres in the 6th arrondissement of Paris. The Centre Sèvres was established in 1974 as a result of the merger of two Jesuit faculties: the Faculté de Philosophie and the Faculté de Théologie. These two faculties had a long-standing history dating back to the 19th century. The Centre Sèvres is part of the worldwide network of Jesuit institutions of higher learning and is affiliated with the Catholic Church. The Centre Sèvres offers a wide range of academic programs, including Bachelor's, Master's, and Doctorate degrees in Philosophy and Theology. The institution also offers specialized programs in social ethics, spirituality, and religious studies. The Centre Sèvres is recognized for its rigorous academic standards and its commitment to the Jesuit tradition of "cura personalis" or "care for the whole person". This approach emphasizes the development of the individual's intellectual, spiritual, and social dimensions. The faculty at Centre Sèvres includes a diverse group of scholars and practitioners from around the world. Many of them are leading experts in their respective fields and contribute to the institution's reputation for academic excellence. The Centre Sèvres also hosts visiting scholars and students from other Jesuit institutions worldwide, fostering a vibrant and diverse academic community. The Centre Sèvres is housed in a historic building that was originally a convent. The building has been renovated to accommodate modern educational facilities while preserving its historic character. The institution's library houses a significant collection of books and manuscripts related to philosophy and theology. The Centre Sèvres plays an active role in the local community and the broader society. It organizes public lectures, seminars, and conferences on a variety of topics related to philosophy, theology, and social issues. The institution also collaborates with other academic institutions, non-profit organizations, and government agencies on research and community service projects. In conclusion, the Centre Sèvres is a distinguished institution of higher learning that combines rigorous academic standards with a commitment to social engagement and personal development. Its unique blend of tradition and innovation makes it a leading center for philosophical and theological studies in France and beyond.

We study the impact of cosmic rays (CRs) on population iii star formation in minihalos, using cosmological hydrodynamic simulations with CR physics implemented self-consistently. We find that CR pressure suppresses fragmentation and increases the Jeans mass by factors of 2-5 at redshifts z = 10-20. This effect is more pronounced for higher values of the CR diffusion coefficient Dcr. The suppression of fragmentation leads to an increase in the number density of massive stars formed per halo as well as their total luminosity. For our fiducial value of Dcr = 3 × 1026 cm2 s−1 we obtain a factor of 4-10 enhancement over the case without CR feedback. Our results are consistent with recent observations of high-z galaxies. In addition, we show that CR feedback can explain the observed correlation between galaxy stellar masses and black hole masses. Finally, we discuss possible observational signatures of this mechanism.

Denise Rosenthal's debut album "Cambio de Piel" was released in 1995 and features several famous songs such as "Adán", "No dejes" and "Bienvenida". The song "Bienvenida" is especially notable as it became the official song for the Mexican citizenship ceremony. The album was awarded a Lo Nuestro award for Pop Album of the Year and a Latin Grammy for Best Female Pop Vocal Album. Sea oflove – Denise Rosenthal Spanish version by Nuria Fergo Marketplace – Denise Rosenthal Adán – Denise Rosenthal No dejes – Denise Rosenthal Bienvenida – Denise Rosenthal The album was released in Japan under the name "Ai no Uta" and in Italy as "Cos'è l'amore". seek Denise Rosenthal's debut album "Cambio de Piel" was released in 1995 and features several famous songs such as "Adán", "No dejes" and "Bienvenida". The song "Bienvenida" is especially notable as it became the official song for the Mexican citizenship ceremony. DRAMATIC PORTRAIT OF A SOUL IN PERIL Denise Rosenthal's voice is distinctive, intense, and full of life. The soul of this singer shines through in her first album "Cambio de Piel", a small album that tells a story of several feelings, emotions and situations that are part of our daily lives. This album, released in 1995, speaks to us with the voice and emotions of a new generation that is experiencing the changes and challenges that our time presents us with. Rosenthal's powerful voice was trained in opera and spans a wide range of emotions. Her voice can soar to lofty heights, but it also has the ability to soften and reach out to the lowest depths. The elements of the dramatic genre are clearly evident in this album. We can clearly hear the rhythms of a particular musical style in the song "Sea of Love", and "Marketplace" has a rhythm similar to that of a samba song. However, Rosenthal has such a wide variety of musical genres and styles that she is able to weave a completely new sound for each of the songs on the album. The lyrics of the songs are written with such poetic beauty that they take on a special meaning that goes beyond just the literal meaning of the words. Rosenthal's vocals and the compositions for this album gave new life to several classic love songs such as "Adán", "No dejes" and "Bienvenida". These songs are about deep passions and strong emotions that cannot be easily expressed with simple words. Rosenthal's powerful voice conveys all of the subtleties of these songs in such a clear and distinct way that they could have been written just for her. We can clearly hear the emotions that the songs are bringing up for her in her voice. The feelings and desires expressed in these songs helped shape this new artist's artistic expression and opened a window into her soul.

This isn't limited to leafleting, although it did include leafleting. In 1954, the CIA waged a campaign of psychological warfare through radio, leafleting, and political postering on the Guatemalan people in order to scare the army into staging a coup. The attempt was successful, despite the fact that the actual land forces of CIA-backed Castillo Armas were small and barring a miracle, would have been defeated by the forces of the Guatemalan government at the time.
Some background: in 1944, the dictator of Guatemala, Jorge Ubico, was overthrown after a decade and a half of rule, and replaced by Guatemala's first democracy. Guatemala's second president, Jacobo Arbenz, initiated a campaign of land reform known as Decree 900 to take and redistribute a portion of the unused land of the United Fruit Company. At this time, the UFC owned 70% of arable land in Guatemala, its only port, and its only railroad. The plan was to give the appropriated land to poor peasants in order to create a basis for economic growth in Guatemala. Under Ubico, the UFC had been paying property taxes based on an undervaluation of their property. Arbenz's government would have compensated the UFC at this valuation. 
The UFC was predictably not happy with this and initiated a campaign with the US government to overthrow Arbenz, on the basis that it was increasing the Communist foothold in South America. The Arbenz government had interacted with the USSR but was not reliant on them and did not align with them. Numerous times they stated that this redistributive policy was only intended to create a basis for capitalist development in their nation.
From Foreign Minister Guillermo Toriello at the Inter-American Conference:
"The delegation of Guatemala is here, its head high, to express the legitimate aspirations of our people and the Revolutionary Government, solidly united in the desire and the effort to achieve an effective political and economic freedom, through the exercise of democracy, absolute respect for the political and social rights of man, and the development of an economic program in accordance with the present and future needs of the nation."
_URL_0_
The whole document is worth checking out. In campaigning the government, the UFC hired publicist Edward Bernays to use his connections in US media to paint a picture of Guatemala as fostering communist development. The Dulles family had close connections to the UFC, being large shareholders in the company, while one was Secretary of Defense* (John Foster Dulles) and the other (Allen Dulles) director of the CIA. They participated in lobbying the government to do something about Jacobo Arbenz.
The CIA threw their weight behind Castillo Armas, and initiated their plan of psychological warfare. Armas invaded with CIA assistance in 1954, with only a force of 480 men. They invaded in such a way as to create the impression among the populace of insurmountable odds for the Guatemalan military. Throughout this invasion, the CIA-sponsored radio station Voice of Liberation broadcast news of a popular right-wing insurgency and the inevitable collapse of the Arbenz government. They dropped leaflets and spread rumors of the inevitable collapse of the Arbenz government, as well as using military force to create the illusion of an imminent invasion. To make a much longer story short, this struck fear into the hearts of the officer corps of the Guatemalan Army, and Arbenz decided that this made his continued existence as president untenable. He resigned in June 1954, and was replaced by the dictator Castillo Armas, who repealed Decree 900 and protected the landholdings of the United Fruit Company.
Source: much of this information came from *Bitter Fruit: The Story of the American Coup in Guatemala* by Stephen Kinzer and Stephen Schlesinger.
I am very open to alternate viewpoints or any challenge to the information I've provided here.
Edit: typo and grammar
*Edit2: /u/Youarenotagangster pointed out that John Foster Dulles was the Secretary of State, not the Secretary of Defense.

There are many benefits to implementing a community service requirement for middle and high school students. For one, it encourages young people to become involved in their communities and develop a sense of responsibility and empathy for others. Community service can also help students to develop valuable skills and knowledge that can benefit them in their future careers and personal lives.
Additionally, requiring community service can help to address social issues that are relevant to young people. This can include volunteering at local shelters or food banks, participating in environmental clean-up initiatives, or working with organizations that support youth advocacy and youth voice. By requiring community service, young people can become more aware of the issues facing their communities and be given the opportunity to make a positive impact.
However, there are also challenges to implementing such a requirement. One of the main concerns is the potential time constraint for students. With already busy schedules due to schoolwork, extracurricular activities, and other obligations, students may struggle to find time to complete their community service hours. To address this challenge, schools could work with students to create a flexible community service plan that works with their individual schedules and accommodates their other commitments. 
Another challenge is ensuring that students remain engaged in their community service activities. It is important to find opportunities that interest students and allow them to connect with their communities in meaningful ways. Schools could work with local organizations to create a range of service options that appeal to different interests and passions. 
By considering these benefits and challenges, schools can create a community service requirement that is meaningful, accessible, and beneficial for both students and their communities. 
Would you like me to generate more ideas to solve the challenges of implementing mandatory community service requirements for students?

This paper analyzes various unsupervised sentence embedding approaches by means of a set of auxiliary prediction tasks. By examining how well classifiers can predict word order, word content, and sentence length, the authors aim to assess how much and what type of information is captured by the different embedding models. The main focus is on a comparison between and encoder-decoder model (ED) and a permutation-invariant model, CBOW. (There is also an analysis of skip-thought vectors, but since it was trained on a different corpus it is hard to compare).
There are several interesting and perhaps counter-intuitive results that emerge from this analysis and the authors do a nice job of examining those results and, for the most part, explaining them. However, I found the discussion of the word-order experiment rather unsatisfying. It seems to me that the appropriate question should have been something like, 'How well does model X do compared to the theoretical upper bound which can be deduced from natural language statistics?' This is investigated from one angle in Section 7, but I would have preferred to the effect of natural language statistics discussed up front rather than presented as the explanation to a 'surprising' observation. I had a similar reaction to the word-order experiments.
Most of the interesting results, in my opinion, are about the ED model. It is fascinating that the LSTM encoder does not seem to rely on natural-language ordering statistics -- it seems like doing so should be a big win in terms of per-parameter expressivity. I also think that it's strange that word content accuracy begins to drop for high-dimensional embeddings. I suppose this could be investigated by handicapping the decoder.
Overall, this is a very nice paper investigating some aspects of the information content stored in various types of sentence embeddings. I recommend acceptance.

Cleansing oneself of prejudices and race-based behaviors is a mental journey that requires self-awareness, acceptance, and a commitment to change. It's about understanding that we are products of the information we receive and that it's challenging to step out of the societal norms we live in. Throughout our lives, we are bombarded with millions of images and bits of information that shape our thinking and stereotypes. We are products of the information given to us, and if someone told us as a child that the world was flat and controlled all the information we received, we would believe it to be true. There is nothing inherently in us that deciphers truth. We must accept that we are filled with bits of false information created by others. We all have prejudices and racial perceptions to varying degrees. By admitting this, we arm ourselves with the most powerful weapon against acting on these prejudices: self-awareness. Having a perception does not mean we will act on it. Understanding that the ideas or information we have received could be false prepares us for the process of finding more truth. We must practice critical thinking, examining the information we receive to guard against faulty ideas. We must understand how deeply false perceptions are absorbed by people in society. These perceptions are not inherent to their thinking but are given to them by others, such as the media. Humans may naturally incline towards prejudices, separating "others" for evolutionary self-protection. We must accept that we are products of our times and just human. Like a person afraid to fly, perceptions can easily change with information and self-awareness. We decide the angle of how we view the information. We must strive to introduce new types of information into our world and new perceptions. Practicing replacement experiences and information or perceptions can help in this journey. By consciously seeking out and absorbing new, diverse information, we can begin to dismantle our prejudices and race-based behaviors, leading to a more inclusive and understanding mindset.

The inverse square law is a principle that expresses the way physical forces or properties spread out from a point source in three-dimensional space. This law is commonly observed in physics, particularly in the fields of gravitation, electrostatics, light and sound propagation, and radiation. The reason why so many phenomena follow the inverse square law is due to the geometry of three-dimensional space. When a physical quantity or force emanates from a point source, it spreads out equally in all directions. If you imagine a sphere centered on the source, the total amount of whatever is spreading out (be it light, sound, gravitational force, etc.) must be spread over the surface of that sphere. As the radius of the sphere (i.e., the distance from the source) increases, the surface area of the sphere increases as the square of the radius (the formula for the surface area of a sphere is 4πr²). Therefore, at twice the distance from the source, the same amount of whatever is spreading out must cover four times the area. At three times the distance, it must cover nine times the area, and so on. This results in the intensity or strength of the force or property decreasing as the inverse square of the distance from the source. Hence, the inverse square law is a natural consequence of the way physical quantities spread out in three-dimensional space.

Drycothaea testacea is a species of beetle in the Dryopidae family that can be found on Sulawesi, Indonesia and possibly other nearby islands such as Borneo (Kalimantan). It was described by Schawaller & Skelley in 2005 from specimens collected at Gunung Mulu National Park near Semporna, Sabah.
The larvae feed on ferns growing under logs or rocks.

Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar is a 2000 Indian English-Hindi bilingual feature film directed by Jabbar Patel. It stars Mammootty in the title role. The film tells the story of B. R. Ambedkar, known mainly for his contributions in the emancipation of the downtrodden and oppressed classes in India and shaping the Constitution of India, as the chairman of the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constituent Assembly. Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar won the National Film Awards for Best feature film in English, Best Actor (Mammootty) and Best Art Direction (Nitin Chandrakant Desai) in 1999. The film was screened retrospective on August 15, 2016 at the Independence Day Film Festival jointly presented by the Indian Directorate of Film Festivals and Ministry of Defence, commemorating 70th Indian Independence Day. Bhimrao Ambedkar studying in Columbia University library is approached by Lala Lajpat Rai to join his home rule league but Ambedkar refuses to do so as he came here on the scholarship of his highness Sayajirao Gaekwad III of Baroda State. Ambedkar is unable to afford studying in America so he also works part-time jobs washing plates and cleaning. He passed his M.A. exam in June 1915, majoring in economics, and other subjects of sociology, history, philosophy and anthropology. He presented a thesis, Ancient Indian Commerce. Ambedkar was influenced by John Dewey and his work on democracy. In 1916 he completed his second thesis, National Dividend of India - A Historic and Analytical Study for another M.A. In October 1916, he enrolled for the Bar course at Gray's Inn, and at the same time enrolled at the London School of Economics where he started working on a doctoral thesis. 1917 The term of his scholarship from Baroda ended, so that he was obliged to go back to India in June with his work unfinished; he was, however, given permission to return and finish within four years. He sent his precious and much-loved collection of books back on a steamer—but it was torpedoed and sunk by a German submarine Ambedkar went to Baroda State to work as a probationer in the Accountant General's Office. A scholarship of 11.50 British pounds a month, for three years, was awarded to the young Ambedkar and per the agreement he has to serve baroda for 10 years after the completion of his studies. However, upon arriving in Baroda, he realized that none of the Hindu hotels would allow his to stay due to his lower caste. He found a Parsi inn, but here, non-Parsis were not allowed to stay. He and the Parsi inn-keeper reached a compromise, where by Ambedkar gave his name as a Parsi, and was allowed to stay. After joining the new office as a new senior officer (Probationary officer) being an untouchable the peon of the office doesn't give him file in his hand he throw files on his table, Ambedkar feel thirsty and ask for a glass of water, the peon says there is no water when he goes on to drink water from the pot, Upper caste people finds this very uncomfortable and insult him and he is not allowed to drink water from that pot as they think by his touch it will pollute the water so they tell him to bring his own water and call him dirty and untouchable. After a few days of stay in Baroda it is discovered by other Parsis, that he is not Parsi and on the eleventh day of his stay, a group of angry Parsi men, armed with sticks, arrived to remove him from the inn. He had to leave the inn that very day, and not having a place to stay, was forced to leave Baroda and return to Bombay to find work elsewhere. In Bombay, Ambedkar applies for the post of professorship as professor of political economy in Sydenham College of Commerce and Economics. On the first day of his job the students makes fun of him thinking what this untouchable will teach them does he know how to speak English. In the staff room when Ambedkar approaches towards water pot to drink water a professor named Trivedi doesn't like it and insults him. Ambedkar is approached by Shri Shahu Maharaj of Kolhapur and Ambedkar starts a newspaper called Mooknayak in the year 1920. Ambedkar took a conference in Mangaon in Kolhapur it was attended by Shri Shahu Maharaj. The Maharaj declared in a prophetic vein "You have found your saviour in Ambedkar and I am confident that he will break your shackles". Sonali Kulkarni as Ramabai Ambedkar, first wife of Ambedkar Mrinal Kulkarni as Savita Ambedkar, second wife of Babasaheb Govind Namdeo as Subedar Ramji Maloji Sakpal, father of Babasaheb Nawazuddin Siddiqui as one of the leaders of Second Mahad Satyagraha and in background of Manusmriti Dahan Sushant Singh as Asnodkar, a friend he met while studying in London Nikhil Ratnaparkhi as Professor Trivedi ( Special Appearance) The film was a project funded jointly by the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment and the Government of Maharashtra. It was made at a budget of 8.95 crore. The production was managed by the government-owned National Film Development Corporation of India. In an interview with Pritish Nandy of Rediff.com, director Jabbar Patel said that the research and filming took three years each. The government said they wanted a film on the scale of Richard Attenborough's Gandhi. For the role of Ambedkar, they considered hundreds of actors around the world. Patel claimed that, in that search, they also sought Robert De Niro who was very keen on the role but backed off when he was told that he had to drop his American accent and speak the way Ambedkar did -- in his typically "clipped Indo-British accent". Mammootty was cast by chance when Patel saw his picture on a magazine and felt he had some resemblance with Ambedkar. Mammootty was initially resistant to be cast as that would have meant shaving off his mustache, recalls Patel. Since there were no footage of Ambedkar available to them, his body language and way of talking was to be done out of imagination. The film was simultaneously shot in English and Hindi. Although Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar was certified in 1998, it was released commercially in 2000. The film won National Film Awards (India) in 1999.

This is inevitably going to be somewhat subjective, but consider the following:
1) There was a time period in 1969-1970 where, for nearly a year, we had an attempting bombing or hijacking carried out by far-left groups *every single day*. During the Cambodian protests, professors who didn't fly peace flags from their houses often had their cars burnt, their houses attacked, etc, and Washington DC was essentially under siege. There were even cases of professors having their lifelong research destroyed in arson or bombing attacks. There was also a pretty nasty risk of right-wing backlash to this, as both Johnson and Nixon very well knew and took into consideration with their Vietnam policies. Wallace was just one prominent manifestation of this. There were times-most prominently in 1968 and in May of 1970-where it looked like the nation was having a nervous breakdown of sorts.
Thankfully, for a host of reasons, it didn't get as bad as it could have, not least because the much maligned Hoover-era FBI was pretty effective at shutting down really nasty far-left and far-right groups without killing anybody. (EDIT: At least for the most part, when you consider Fred Hampton.)
2) Whole swathes of cities were burnt down in the 1968 riots-two whole miles around Madison Street in Chicago, for instance. Some, like the area around 14th Street in Washington D C, didn't recover for multiple decades. Urban warfare was a very real possibility, and on a very limited scale, it did happen a fair amount in the late 1960s and early 1970s.
3) Aside from Vietnam, we were dealing with a *lot* of very nasty foreign policy problems, including a USSR that had just reached nuclear parity with the US and a deeply... "world revolution phase", isolated Cultural Revolution-era China (think basically the North Korea of the day). They nearly engaged in nuclear conflict in 1969. Plenty of trouble in the rest of the world-Middle East, stability of NATO, et all-as well. Back in DC, the whole consensus behind US foreign policy in WWII was collapsing in the late 1960s.
4) I'll let someone else speak for LBJ, but Nixon's paranoia about enemies stretched back to the beginning of his Presidency. Nor was it fully ungrounded in reality: even paranoids have enemies, and Nixon certainly had a lot of them. Nixon's blood feud with some of the "best" circles in the US stretched back to the early 1950s, and had only escalated throughout the years. *This doesn't change the fact that Nixon took care to inflame those numbers by his own actions as a President*, but as I've mentioned in a previous post here, the root of the conflict between Nixon and "the Establishment"-or at least certain sections of it-stretched beyond simple policy differences. Much like Bill Clinton and the swathes of the nation that hated him, there probably was nothing Nixon could have done to make them *not* go after him. Nixon and Clinton both certainly could have avoided inflaming the numbers of their enemies by not treating professional civil servants and related figures who might never have liked them or voted for them, but would have respected the office, as pre-emptive enemies: but the existence of their hard core gut cultural haters, the types that would have rather seen the nation suffer than them succeed, that was a fait accompli before they took office, and a degree of paranoia was probably just mere realism.
All in all, for all the above stated reasons and more, I'd say that the paranoia of people who had the ill luck of running the USA in the late 1960s was quite grounded in reality. I'm of the opinion that Nixon, Humphrey, RFK, Reagan, and Rockefeller all deserve credit for even wanting to be POTUS in 1968.
Sources:
Stephen Ambrose: Nixon, Volume II.
Rick Perlstein: Nixonland.
Even Thomas: Being Nixon.
Jussi Hanhimaki: Flawed Architect.
Dan Berger: Outlaws of America.
Dave Gergen's Memoirs.
EDIT:
Here's a story that will slam my point on home: the first thing Richard Nixon was given upon assuming office was a stack of forms from the Johnson Administration. All Nixon had to do was pen in the name of the city and his signature, and martial law would be immediately declared in that city. **It really was that bad.** '
LBJ mentioned in his memoirs that he considered it a miracle that there was not mass anarchy in the inner cities upon Nixon's inauguration and term in office.

The song was written by Jimmy Webb and recorded in 1967 as the B-side to The Beatles' single, Hey Jude.
It reached number one on Billboard's Hot 100 chart during August 1968 after being released as a single from their album, Let It Be. 
 
 Short fuse blues is about a man who has had enough of his wife nagging him. He wants her out but doesn't want to leave himself without any income so he decides that she should work at home instead (as opposed to working outside). This way they can both be happy because he'll have more time to spend with her while she's still around when he's not working. 
This version features Ringo Starr playing drums along with Paul McCartney singing lead vocals.

Italian dressing is a vinaigrette-type salad dressing in American cuisine that consists of water, vinegar or lemon juice, vegetable oil, chopped bell peppers, sugar or corn syrup, herbs and spices (including oregano, fennel, dill and salt) and sometimes onion and garlic. The creamy Italian variant adds milk products and stabilizers. Italian and creamy Italian dressings are often bought bottled, or prepared by mixing oil and vinegar with a packaged flavoring mix consisting of dehydrated vegetables and herbs. Despite its name, Italian dressing is not used in Italy, where salad is normally dressed with olive oil, vinegar or lemon juice, salt, and sometimes balsamic vinegar at the table, and not with a pre-mixed vinaigrette. In North American cuisine, Italian dressing is also used as a marinade for meat or vegetables, for stir-frys, and on sandwiches. Pasta salads sometimes include Italian dressing. The caloric content of Italian dressing varies widely. North American style Italian salad dressing is thought to date back to 1941 in Framingham, Massachusetts. Florence Hanna, the daughter of Italian immigrants and wife of restaurateur Ken Hanna, made large batches of her family's salad dressing for the house salads at the restaurant. The restaurant, originally called The '41 Cafe, later changed locations and became known as Ken's Steak House. The salad dressing became so popular that it was made in large vats in the basement of Ken's to keep up with demand. Customers started requesting the Italian dressing to go. The Crowley family, who owned a nearby manufacturing business, approached the Hanna family about bottling the popular salad dressing. This resulted in the creation of Ken's Salad Dressing, which now comes in several variations of Italian, as well as many other flavors. Italian salad dressing was also served in Kansas City, Missouri, at the Wishbone Restaurant beginning in 1948. The Wishbone had been opened in 1945 by Phillip Sollomi along with Lena Sollomi, Phillip's mother. The Italian dressing served at the Wishbone was based on a recipe from Lena Sollomi's Sicilian family which was a blend of oil, vinegar, herbs and spices. Demand for the salad dressing proved so high that Phillip started a separate operation to produce it for sale, making it by the barrel. The dressing recipe was eventually purchased by Lipton and has been made commercially by a succession of owners since, generating an entire Wish-Bone brand of products currently made by Pinnacle Foods, Inc.

We study the geometry of generalized Gaussian distribution (GGD) in terms of its Fisher information matrix and entropy, which are closely related to each other by Cramér-Rao inequality. We show that GGDs with different parameters have different geometries; specifically, we prove that for any fixed parameter $\mu$, as $\sigma \to 0$ or $+\infty$, the Fisher information matrix converges to zero while the entropy diverges to infinity. This is consistent with our intuition on how small variance and large variance affect the uncertainty of data points. Finally, we provide an algorithm to compute the exact value of the Fisher information matrix for any given GGD. The results presented here can be used to analyze the performance of many machine learning algorithms based on GGD such as kernel density estimation and support vector machines. Keywords: Generalized Gaussian Distribution, Entropy, Fisher Information Matrix, Geometric Analysis, Machine Learning

We use a series of N-body/smoothed particle hydrodynamics simulations andanalytic arguments to show that the presence of an effective temperature floorin the interstellar medium at T_F ~ 10^4 K naturally explains the tendency forlow-mass galaxies to be more spheroidal, more gas rich, and less efficient inconverting baryons into stars than larger galaxies. The trend arises becausegas pressure support becomes important compared to angular momentum support insmall dark matter haloes. We suggest that dwarf galaxies with rotationalvelocities ~ 40 km/s do not originate as thin discs, but rather are born asthick, puffy systems. If accreted on to larger haloes, tenuous dwarfs of thiskind will be more susceptible to gas loss or tidal transformation thanscaled-down versions of larger spirals. For a constant temperature floor,pressure support becomes less important in large haloes, and this produces atendency for massive isolated galaxies to have thinner discs and more efficientstar formation than their less massive counterparts, as observed.

In fact there are some papers that have already discussed about it but they did not give any solution to solve these problems. 
This article gives an overview how humans perceive images from different angles such as color, shape etc., also discusses several issues related with human visual system (HVS) like contrast sensitivity function(CSF), spatial frequency response curve(SFRC).

I'm not the biggest fan of imperial atrocity competition--it's not like the (for example) Mexican Inquisition was the kinder, cuddlier inquisition. But there's no doubt that the Portuguese saw the cosmopolitanism of India as a major threat to their economic and religious ambitions, and reacted with sometimes stunning levels of violence.
The Portuguese push to round the Cape of Good Hope was an attempted end-run around Muslims' domination of Anatolia and the Near East. They were not too happy, you can imagine, to discover that the eastern African coastline south of Ethiopia was controlled by the powerful *Islamic* Swahili city-states; nor were they pleased to find Muslims along with "pagans" (Hindus) and Thomas Christians in India and offshore islands. It's become more popular in recent scholarship to talk about 16th century Iberian imperialism as a type of crusade, and India is no exception. The sort of apocalyptic Christian utopianism united with imperial economic-political ambitions had devastating effects in India, particularly the colonial core in Goa. 
Afonso de Albuquerque, who led the European conquest and installed himself as the first Duke of Goa, pretty much broke in his office by slaughtering all the Muslim men he could find. As for the women? Well, Muslim and Hindu women alike, the Portuguese decreed, would be married off to incoming Portuguese men to help secure their conversions to Christianity and produce a next generation of native Indian Christians.
The next decades saw waves of mass conversion or "conversion" through public ritual, mob persuasion, basically making Christianity the best option for people. Or the only option: there was a *massive* campaign of Hindu iconoclasm by European Christians, destroying countless temples and shrines. A good number of churches were built either on top of or out of the rubble of former temples and mosques.
Goa suffered even more than other areas of India. One of the patterns that Ines Zupanov and other scholars of Jesuit/Catholic missionary activity have pointed out is that the further away from a center of imperial political control, the more tolerant or flexible missionaries tended to be. Goa was Portuguese ground zero, and members of European religious orders (think Franciscans, rather than your average parish priest) kept it under tight lockdown. Even with the establishment of a seminary to train and ordain Indian priests, the Europeans locked them out of any position that might earn them a living or any sort of political/spiritual power. 
With all of this background, you, gentle reader, probably *were* expecting the Goa Inquisition, and sure enough it was called and then legislated into being on the advice of *Saint* Francis Xavier in 1560. Both the delay and the formation of the inquisitorial office had the same reason. By that point, the number of Indian Christians and "New Christian" (conversos or from converso families, that is, Iberian Jews who had forcibly converted to Christianity) immigrants had reached a density to trigger the Catholic religious orders' (Franciscans especially) and secular leaders' paranoia that *they weren't actually Christian*. The Goa Inquisition investigated tens of thousands of Indians and put over 16,000 of them on trial. Most of the Inquisition records were destroyed in the 19th century. There is mention of around 4200 who were punished in some way; 57 of them were burned alive.
The Portuguese attempt to Christianize its territory in India, overall, has very little to recommend it to posterity.

Shopping for college can be an overwhelming task, but with the right approach, you can make it a breeze. Here's how: 1. Get your textbook list straight from the source: Contact your professors or check the syllabus to know exactly what books you'll need. 2. Skip the campus bookstore and shop online: Websites like Amazon and Chegg often offer textbooks at a fraction of the price. 3. Consider an old edition: Older editions often contain the same information and are significantly cheaper. 4. Rent your textbooks: If you don't plan on keeping your textbooks, consider renting them. 5. Check for coupons: Websites often offer discounts or coupons, so be sure to check before making a purchase. 6. Share the cost with a dependable friend: If you have a friend in the same class, consider splitting the cost of the textbook. 7. Buy from upperclassmen: They may have the books you need and sell them at a lower price. 8. Check the cost of international editions: These are often cheaper and contain the same information. 9. Stock up on writing utensils: You'll need plenty of pens, pencils, and highlighters. 10. Buy supplies that keep your notes organized: Consider investing in binders, folders, and notebooks. 11. Keep your dorm desk organized: Purchase desk organizers to keep your workspace tidy. 12. Invest in a good computer and other tech supplies: A reliable laptop is a must. Also consider a printer, external hard drive, and USB sticks. 13. Find out the bed size before you buy: Dorm beds often require specific sheet sizes, so check before buying. 14. Take a good look at yourself: Invest in a full-length mirror to ensure you always look your best. 15. Get up on time: A reliable alarm clock is a must. 16. Know what clothes to bring: Check the weather and dress code of your college before packing. 17. Clear storage space: Purchase storage bins or shelves to keep your dorm room organized. 18. Decorate your space: Posters, photos, and fairy lights can make your dorm room feel more like home. 19. Buy more luggage, if necessary: You'll need sturdy luggage to transport your belongings. 20. Buy shower supplies: Don't forget essentials like towels, shower shoes, and a shower caddy. 21. Take care of your hair: Bring along your favorite hair care products and tools. 22. Make yourself presentable: Invest in a good skincare routine and basic makeup essentials. 23. Bring along some first aid items: Band-aids, pain relievers, and cold medicine are must-haves. 24. Stay healthy: Stock up on vitamins and healthy snacks. 25. Know what you will be responsible for cleaning: If you're responsible for cleaning your own space, make sure you have the necessary supplies. 26. Make sure you can clean the floor: A small vacuum or broom will come in handy. 27. Purchase laundry supplies: Don't forget detergent, fabric softener, and a laundry bag. 28. Keep germs at bay: Hand sanitizer and disinfecting wipes are essential. 29. Stock up on movies and music: These will help you relax and unwind. 30. Get a good pair of headphones: These are essential for studying and relaxing. 31. Bring books you actually enjoy: You'll need some downtime, so bring along your favorite books. 32. Buy games and sports supplies: These can help you bond with your new friends. 33. Know what you need and what you can bring: Check your college's rules before packing. 34. Shop for a range of food storage containers: These will come in handy for leftovers and snacks. 35. Get your essential utensils: Don't forget things like a can opener, bottle opener, and cutlery. 36. Mind your dishes: You'll need plates, bowls, cups, and mugs. Remember, college is a new chapter in your life. Make sure you're prepared, but also remember to enjoy the experience.

Frieston is a small village in the East Riding of Yorkshire, England. The village is situated just south of the River Aupth under the route of the A161. To the west is the village of Ar wystwiel, to the north is Great Chesters and to the east are the towns of Howden and Wortley. Frieston Hall was the home of the Harrison-Calvert family for six generations. The current Hall was built in 1714 and many of the out buildings date from this time. The hall is a Grade II* listed building and it is this building that is open to the public. In 1801 the population of Frieston was 113 people. It grew slowly over the next century, reaching a peak of 213 in 1911. After this the population began to decline and by 1951 the population was just 101. Since the 1970's however, the population of Frieston has grown steadily and currently the village has a population of about 320. Frieston is home to one school, Frieston CofE School, which is named after Sir Isaac Newton and which opened in 1847. The school rolls are maintained by Ofsted and the school has a rating of "Outstanding". The current headteacher is Mrs Karen Radford. Frieston is located close to several transport links. The A1(M) passes to the south of the village and so does the Ribble Link motorway which is part of the London to Leeds artery. To the north and west is the A161 and to the east is the rail network which connects Frieston to both Leeds and Howden. Frieston Hall, Frieston CofE School and the surrounding area are home to a number of well known artists. These include portrait painter Angela Smith, landscape painter David Scroggie and wood-engraver Karel Komrske.

How to Pickle Peppers
Pickling is a simple way to preserve food, and it can add a salty, sour, or sweet taste to almost any vegetable. 
Ingredients:
1 cup white vinegar
1 cup sugar
1/2 teaspoon pickling salt
1/2 teaspoon celery seed
1/2 teaspoon mustard seed
1/2 teaspoon turmeric, or curry powder
1/8 teaspoon ground cinnamon
4 cups sliced, fresh hot peppers, such as banana, jalapeno, or Anaheim
Instructions:
In a large saucepan, bring the vinegar, sugar, salt, celery seed, mustard seed, turmeric, and cinnamon to a boil over medium-high heat, stirring frequently.
Pack the hot peppers into 2 clean, hot pint jars, leaving 1/2 inch of space between the tops of the peppers and the rims of the jars.
Pour the hot pickling liquid over the peppers in the jars, leaving 1/2 inch of space from the rims of the jars.
Wipe the rims of the jars with a clean, damp cloth. 
Place the jars in a single layer in a boiling water canner. 
Add enough water to cover the jars by 1 inch.
Bring the water to a boil.
Process the jars for 10 minutes.
Turn off the heat, remove the jars from the water, and let them cool undisturbed for 12 to 14 hours.
Store the jars in a cool, dark place for up to 1 year.

Viktor Esbensen (born September 16, 1974) is the former mayor of Hamar municipality in Norway and leader of the Progress Party from 2005 to 2009.
Esbensen was born on September 16, 1974 in Trondheim as the son of politician Per-Olaf Esbensen who served as Mayor of Trondheim between 1989 and 1995. He graduated from University College London where he studied Political Science before returning home to work at his father's company.

Your answer is practically in your title: they were trials. Courts of law were, of course, recorded for reference by a note taker.[(See these transcripts edited by Paul Boyer and Stephen Nissenbaum)](_URL_1_)
We can also attribute some of the more personal records thanks to high literacy rates in the colonies in comparison with Europe. [The book *Literacy in the United States: Readers and Reading Since 1880* gives us a good background of the idea of these literacy rates, putting them at about 60% in 1650.](_URL_0_)
As it was largely seen as an ecclesiastical matter of the human soul, witch craft being seen as a corrupting and evil nature, priests and the religiously devoted delivered sermons at mass on the subject, encouraging religious devotion and purity through God. Their commentaries and record taking are, of course, always quite good for first-hand accounts of events. [Samuel Parris, for example, gives a few sermons with the express idea of 'devils' AKA witches, within the church.](_URL_2_)
So, overall, its thanks to this trifecta that we have such good records: high literacy rates amongst the public, court records, and ecclesiastical records all help up research the trials today. 
If you're looking for more documentation on the issue, or current scholars interpretations of the events, check out the University of Chicago's [well compiled list of resources.] (_URL_3_)
It is a general reminder, however, to try and stay impartial to the primary source material of first-hand accounts of the info above. Not all priests, law makers, or lay people were impartial to these cases, and their feelings on the issue may have transferred into their records. Keep an objective view when reading!

Building a bridge with popsicle sticks is a fun and educational project that can be done at home or in a classroom setting. Here's how to do it: 1. Determine the length of your bridge: The length of your bridge will depend on the number of popsicle sticks you have and the size of the gap you want to span. 2. Gather your supplies: You will need popsicle sticks, a ruler, a pencil, and glue. You may also need a pair of scissors or a small saw to cut the sticks to the right length. 3. Ready your work space: Clear a flat surface and lay down some newspaper or a drop cloth to catch any glue drips. 4. Decide on the kind of bridge you will make: There are many types of bridges, but a truss bridge is a good choice for beginners. 5. Imagine a blueprint for your bridge: Think about how your bridge will look and how the pieces will fit together. 6. Draw your blueprint: Use your ruler and pencil to draw a detailed plan of your bridge on a piece of paper. 7. Plan out your truss beam layout: The truss beams are the diagonal pieces that give the bridge its strength. Plan where these will go on your blueprint. 8. Glue your truss beams together: Apply a small amount of glue to the ends of the popsicle sticks and press them together. Hold them in place until the glue dries. 9. Lay out your trusses and let the glue set: Arrange the truss beams on your blueprint and let the glue dry completely. 10. Measure your cross supports: These are the horizontal pieces that connect the truss beams. Measure and cut these to the right length. 11. Unite the beams of your trusses: Glue the cross supports to the truss beams. 12. Lay out the deck of your bridge: This is the flat surface that cars or people would travel on. Lay out popsicle sticks side by side and glue them together. 13. Anchor your deck with staggered lengthwise supports: These are the long pieces that run the length of the bridge. Glue these to the underside of the deck. 14. Measure for cradle supports and truss joiners: These are the pieces that connect the deck to the trusses. Measure and cut these to the right length. 15. Cut your cradle supports and truss joiners: Use your scissors or saw to cut these pieces. 16. Unite the truss walls: Glue the cradle supports and truss joiners to the deck and the trusses. 17. Lay additional deck support beams as needed: If your bridge is long, you may need additional support beams. 18. Attach your deck: Glue the deck to the trusses. 19. Add upper support beams: If you have long popsicle sticks, lay these across the top of the struts and glue them into place. With patience and careful planning, you can build a sturdy and beautiful bridge out of popsicle sticks. Happy building!

The presidential election procedure in the United States is a topic of much debate and the country's electoral system is frequently chastised for neglecting the will of the people. The popular vote, which refers to the total number of votes received by a candidate or measure throughout the entire country, is one method that has gained attention as a possibly more democratic alternative to the present system.
The popular vote approach promotes inclusivity and ensures that the perspectives of various populations throughout the United States are heard. It gives every citizen, regardless of where they live or how densely populated their state is, an equal say in the election process. This is significant because it may encourage greater voter engagement and lessen the impact of voters in more populous states on the election outcome.
Furthermore, the popular vote method assures that the elected leader is accountable to the people as a whole. The candidate who receives the most votes across the country is clearly favored by the public, and the President must consequently serve the interests of all citizens. This helps to maintain a sense of democracy and prevents partisan interests from dominating the political landscape.
It is critical to address the issue of keeping the public pleased and content with their elected officials. The popular vote method is an excellent way to ensure that the wishes of the people are represented in the election of the President of the United States. This method guarantees that every voter has an equal say in the election process, promotes inclusivity, and holds the elected leader accountable to the people. Adoption of this method would demonstrate a true commitment to democratic values and ideals in the United States electoral process.

The Face On Mars Controversy The debate over whether there is intelligent life beyond Earth has been ongoing for decades now. In 1976, astronomer Carl Edward Sagan published his book Cosmic Connection where he stated that "we are all made of star stuff" (Sagan). This means we share common origins as well as similar chemical compositions which can be found throughout our solar system including other planets such as Mercury, Venus, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune, Pluto, comets, asteroids, moons, etc. Since then many people have speculated about the possibility of extra-terrestrial intelligence existing elsewhere within this vast universe. There was much excitement when amateur photographer David Whitehouse claimed to find what appeared to be a face carved into the surface of Mars during one of NASA's rover missions back in 1996. Magyar Posse is a Finnish post-rock band from Pori, Finland. The band was formed in 2000 and is known for its unique blend of cinematic post-rock and electronic music. The band's name, Magyar Posse, is a reference to the Hungarian people, known as Magyars, and the term 'posse', which is often used to refer to a group or team. Magyar Posse was formed by Marko Väisänen, Pasi Salmi, and Jani Lehto. The band's initial lineup also included Kalle Tolonen and Tomi Leppänen. The band's music is characterized by its instrumental compositions, which often feature long, atmospheric build-ups and climaxes. The band's sound has been compared to that of other post-rock bands such as Godspeed You! Black Emperor and Mogwai. Magyar Posse released their debut album, "We Will Carry You Over the Mountains," in 2002. The album was well received by critics and helped establish the band's reputation in the post-rock scene. The band's second album, "Kings of Time," was released in 2004 and was also well received. The album's title track, "Kings of Time," is often cited as one of the band's best songs. In 2006, Magyar Posse released their third album, "Random Avenger." The album marked a departure from the band's previous sound, incorporating more electronic elements. Despite the change in sound, the album was well received by critics. Magyar Posse has performed at numerous music festivals and concerts throughout Europe. Despite their success, the band has remained relatively unknown outside of the post-rock scene. The band announced an indefinite hiatus in 2008, but they have occasionally reunited for special performances. Magyar Posse's music has been praised for its cinematic quality, with many critics noting its suitability for film soundtracks. Indeed, the band's music has been used in several films, including the 2006 Finnish film "Sauna." Despite their hiatus, Magyar Posse continues to have a significant influence on the post-rock genre. Their unique blend of post-rock and electronic music has inspired numerous other bands and musicians.
