Andy Karl (born August 28, 1974 as Andrew Karl Cesewski) is an American actor and singer, best known for performing in musical theatre. He appeared on Broadway in the original productions of the musical version of Groundhog Day (for which he won the Olivier Award, Drama Desk Award and Outer Critics Circle Award), Legally Blonde, 9 to 5, Rocky the Musical and Pretty Woman: The Musical, as well as appearing in Wicked (in which he played Fiyero in 2010), The Mystery of Edwin Drood, Jersey Boys, On The Twentieth Century (for which he won the Outer Critics Circle Award) and And So It Goes. Karl was born on August 28, 1974 in Baltimore, Maryland to Walter Cesewski and Susan Weisman. He attended Towson High School, where he was on the football team and performed in theater. In high school, he played Jud Fry in Oklahoma! and General Bullmoose in Li'l Abner. At age 16 he performed as the title role in Aladdin at the White Marsh Dinner Theatre. After high school, he attended Towson University, where he studied voice and music. Karl originally planned on becoming a voice teacher until the director of a show he was in at college told him he had what it took to be a professional actor. Karl continued to perform in shows while in college. He changed his name to Karl to honor his late great-grandfather, Andrew Karl, a Baltimore fireman. In Winter 1993–1994, he played the 9-foot dragon in the children's musical The Reluctant Dragon at Toby's Dinner Theatre in Columbia, Maryland. Karl moved to New York in 1994. He was an understudy in the touring cast of The Who's Tommy and in 1998 played Rum Tum Tugger in the touring cast of Cats. In February 2000 Karl was an understudy in the New York premiere of Sondheim's Saturday Night at the Second Stage Theatre. He made his Broadway debut in 2000 as the replacement for Joey in Saturday Night Fever. He continued with the production, this time in the role of Tony Manero, in its first national tour. In 2001 he performed in the musical stage production Me and Mrs. Jones at the Prince Music Theater in Philadelphia. Karl was next seen as Dino Del Canto in the world premiere of the musical Romeo and Bernadette at the Coconut Grove Playhouse in Miami, Florida. The show, which ran in January 2003, garnered Karl a Carbonell Award for Best Actor in a musical. He reprised the role at the Paper Mill Playhouse in Millburn, New Jersey in February and March, 2003. Karl was also seen at the Paper Mill Playhouse in June and July of that year, when he performed as Danny Zuko in Grease. In January 2004 he performed in the musical revue Nights on Broadway II at Caesar's Palace in Atlantic City, New Jersey. In September 2004, Karl played Luke in the premiere of Altar Boyz at the New York Musical Theatre Festival. He then left the New York area to tour in the original company of Disney's On the Record. Karl returned to New York to play Luke in the Off-Broadway production of Altar Boyz, which opened on March 1, 2005 at Stage 4 of Dodger Stages (now New World Stages). On September 26, 2005, Karl performed in the Actor's Fund Concert production of On the Twentieth Century. Karl's next role was the title character Adam Patterson in the Off-Broadway musical Slut at the American Theatre of Actors in Fall 2005. In January 2006 Karl was in the three-person cast of Hunka Hunka Burnin' Love, a celebration of the music of Elvis Presley, at the now-closed Au Bar. In June and July of that year, he starred as Tad in the musical Bright Lights, Big City at the Prince Music Theater in Philadelphia. He then joined the Broadway cast of The Wedding Singer at the Al Hirschfeld Theatre, where he played the roles of Drunk Dave and Bad Haircut Guy until the show closed at the end of 2006. Karl's next role was in Legally Blonde, where he played Kyle "the UPS guy", Dewey, and Grandmaster Chad at the Golden Gate Theatre in San Francisco, where the musical had its pre-Broadway tryout from January 23 to February 24, 2007. He reprised his roles in the Broadway production, which started previews on April 3 and opened on April 29, 2007 at the Palace Theatre. He was also an understudy to Emmett and Professor Callahan. He left the show on July 20, 2008. Karl briefly left Legally Blonde in June 2007 to work on 9 to 5 in its week-long workshop. After leaving Legally Blonde, Karl continued working on the musical 9 to 5. He played Joe, the love interest of Allison Janney's character, in the pre-Broadway tryout in Los Angeles. The show played at the Ahmanson Theatre from September 3 to October 19, 2008. The show opened on Broadway on April 30, 2009 at the Marquis Theatre, and closed on September 6, 2009. Karl took over the role of Fiyero from Kevin Kern in the Broadway production of Wicked on February 2, 2010 and played the role until January 2, 2011. He took over the role Tommy Devito in the Broadway production of Jersey Boys on October 11, 2011. Karl played Neville Landless in the Broadway revival of The Mystery of Edwin Drood which opened on November 29, 2012 at Studio 54. For this performance, he was nominated for the Drama Desk Award for Outstanding Featured Actor in a Musical. Karl and Margo Seibert were featured as Rocky Balboa and Adrian in the new Lynn Ahrens and Stephen Flaherty musical Rocky the Musical, which opened on Broadway in February 2014. In order to get into shape to play Rocky Balboa, Karl put on 12 pounds of muscle and worked with a trainer to get to a heavyweight fighter's weight. Karl received a nomination for the Tony Award for Best Leading Actor in a Musical for this performance. Karl played the role of Bruce Granit in the 2015 Broadway revival of On the Twentieth Century alongside Kristin Chenoweth in the role of Lily Garland/Mildred Plotka. He received a nomination for the Tony Award for Best Featured Actor in a Musical for his performance. In 2015, Karl joined the recurring cast of Law & Order: Special Victims Unit as the unit's new sergeant, Mike Dodds, who is the son of Peter Gallagher's Chief William Dodds. The role reunites Karl and Gallagher after working together on On the Twentieth Century. He left his role on Law & Order: Special Victims Unit, when his character died. On January 28, 2016, it was announced that Karl would be playing the role of Phil in the musical adaptation of Groundhog Day, the role played in the 1993 movie by Bill Murray. The musical ran at the Old Vic Theatre London from Monday 11 July 2016 - Saturday 17 September 2016. Karl then reprised the role in the Broadway production of Groundhog Day, which opened on April 17, 2017. For his performance in Groundhog Day in London, he received the 2017 Olivier Award for Best Actor in a Musical. On August 15, 2017, the producers of Groundhog Day announced it would end its Broadway run on September 17, 2017, but that a national tour and a possible return to London would follow. In May 2018, it was announced that Karl would star as Edward Lewis in the Broadway production of Pretty Woman: The Musical, following Steve Kazee's departure. Karl met his wife Orfeh while in the musical for Saturday Night Fever. They were married in January 2001 and reside in Manhattan. In addition to Saturday Night Fever, Karl and his wife have performed together in Me and Mrs. Jones, Nights on Broadway II, Bright Lights, Big City, Legally Blonde, and Pretty Woman: The Musical.

The Banzai charge was a military tactic used by Japanese soldiers during World War II. It was a last-ditch, often suicidal attack, where soldiers would charge at the enemy en masse, usually after a shout of "Tenno Heika Banzai" (Long live the Emperor). The effectiveness of the Banzai charge is a matter of debate. From a tactical perspective, it was often ineffective, leading to heavy casualties and rarely achieving significant military objectives. However, from a psychological perspective, it could be seen as effective in certain situations. The ferocity and determination of the Banzai charges could be terrifying to enemy soldiers, potentially causing them to panic or retreat. The Japanese military command did not necessarily believe that Banzai charges were an effective strategy in terms of winning battles or wars. However, they were seen as a way to uphold the Bushido code of honor, which emphasized bravery, loyalty, and the willingness to die for the Emperor. The Banzai charge was seen as a way for a soldier to die honorably, rather than being captured or surrendering. In the later stages of the war, when Japan was facing defeat, Banzai charges became more common, not because they were seen as an effective strategy, but because they were seen as a preferable alternative to surrender. The Japanese military command, influenced by the Bushido code, often preferred to fight to the death rather than surrender. So, while the Japanese military may not have believed that Banzai charges were an effective strategy in a conventional military sense, they were seen as a way to uphold honor and morale, and to instill fear in the enemy.

The authors present an interesting study on how to improve neural language models (NLMs) for text generation tasks such as machine translation.
Their main idea is that NLMs are often trained with large amounts of data but only use small portions at test time. They propose using attention mechanisms which allow them to focus more on relevant parts during training while still being able to generate reasonable output from unseen input sentences. 
This work has several important contributions including showing that: 1) The proposed method can be used effectively across different NLM architectures; 2) It improves performance significantly over previous methods when tested against standard datasets like WMT'14 English-German news-commentary dataset; 3) It also shows improved results compared to other state-of-the-art approaches based on reinforcement learning techniques. 
Overall this research makes significant progress towards improving current NLM systems.

How to Get Hired
Looking for a new job can be a stressful and time-consuming experience. If you're looking to get employed quickly, you may benefit from using a staffing agency. If you're looking for a specific job or career, you may benefit from using a recruiting firm.
Use a staffing agency
Staffing agencies are designed to help companies find employees to fill short-term or temporary jobs. Many staffing agencies also help people find full-time, long-term jobs. Using a staffing agency can save you time and energy spent on finding a job, as well as provide you with the help of a recruiting professional. Staffing agencies have a large network of potential employers, and they can often find jobs for people more quickly than they could on their own.
Recruitment agencies
A recruiting agency is designed to help companies find employees for specific, full-time jobs. These jobs are often in a specific industry or field. If you're looking for a career change or a job in a specific field, you may benefit from using a recruiting firm. Using a recruiting agency can save you time and energy spent on finding a job, as well as provide you with the help of a recruiting professional. Recruiting agencies have a large network of potential employers, and they can often find jobs for people more quickly than they could on their own.

This paper presents a comprehensive study of the infrared environments of masers associated with star formation. We have utilized data from various infrared surveys to investigate the physical conditions and properties of these environments. Our analysis includes a detailed examination of the spatial distribution of masers, their associated molecular clouds, and the young stellar objects within these regions. We also explore the correlation between maser emission and infrared properties of the surrounding environment. Our results provide significant insights into the role of masers in star formation processes and their potential as diagnostic tools for studying the early stages of stellar evolution. This study contributes to our understanding of the complex interplay between masers, star formation, and the interstellar medium.

Making a healthy strawberry banana smoothie is a simple and delicious way to start your day. Here's how to do it: 1. Consider preparing your ingredients the night before. This will save you time in the morning and ensure that your smoothie is as fresh as possible. 2. Cut the strawberries into small pieces. This will make them easier to blend and ensure that your smoothie has a smooth texture. 3. Peel and slice the banana. This will also make it easier to blend and give your smoothie a creamy texture. 4. Consider cooking your oatmeal the night before. This will make it easier to blend and give your smoothie a hearty texture. 5. Measure out your milk and divide it into two separate cups. This will ensure that your smoothie has the right consistency. 6. Consider gathering some extra ingredients. These could include protein powder, chia seeds, or flax seeds. 7. Pull out your blender and plug it in. Make sure it's clean and ready to go. 8. Pour half of your milk into the blender. This will help to blend the ingredients smoothly. 9. Add the dry ingredients. These could include the oatmeal, protein powder, chia seeds, or flax seeds. 10. Add the peanut butter. This will give your smoothie a rich, nutty flavor. 11. Add the fresh and frozen fruit. This will give your smoothie a sweet, fruity flavor. 12. Pour in the rest of the milk. This will help to blend the ingredients smoothly. 13. Blend until smooth. This should take about 1-2 minutes. 14. Pour into a cup. Your smoothie is now ready to enjoy! 15. Consider adding more ingredients to your smoothie. These could include cacao powder, coconut water, ground flax seed, ginger, honey, kale, spices, or wheat grass. These will add extra flavor and health benefits to your smoothie. Remember, the key to a great smoothie is using fresh, high-quality ingredients. Enjoy your healthy strawberry banana smoothie!

Technology and its uses have greatly evolved over time. One hundred years ago, the concept of the radio was just being introduced while today we have forms of portable communication such as phones, tablets and laptops. As this technology advances, so do its uses. These types of technologies have increasing use in businesses and in schools. With the new developments of technology in schools comes the debate over whether or not students should be able to attend their classes online and at home instead of at school. Students would benefit from being able to attend classes from home as seen through the different environment the access to supplies the freedom it provides. First, working on schoolwork at home vs. in school creates two very different environments. While the school environment is crowded and strict, the home environment is more comfortable. As a student who works on my schoolwork both in and out of school, I find that I am able to focus better when at home. At school I am distracted by friends, conversations, and other students, while at home I am able to focus more on my schoolwork than the distractions around me. My home environment is also much more comfortable than school, which clears my mind and helps me focus as well. Because some students are able to learn more efficiently in a more comfortable environment, the option to take classes online would greatly benefit their education. A student who is more focused and able to work hard on their schoolwork generally gets better grades, which will open up more possibilities for his or her future. While the home environment can be easier to focus in, it also allows a greater access to supplies At school, students have to bring the supplies that they want to use. However, this can be a problem because of limited space and forgetfulness. Many friends of mine often ask if I have certain supplies such as a stapler, colored pencils, or erasers. While I sometimes do have these supplies, I don't always because of the limited space in my backpack. However, taking classes online at home would eliminate this problem. Instead of having to remember and worrying about having enough space to bring supplies to and from school, students could always have the supplies available in their own home. This way, students also wouldn't have to worry about forgetting notebooks, textbooks, or folders at home when they need them for school because the supplies would always be in one place. Finally, taking classes at home online would offer students the freedom to work at their own pace In the classroom, different students have different abilities and different levels of understanding in various subjects. For example, while I tend to have a higher understanding in math, some of my friends do better in history and others in science. However, at school we are all forced to work at the same pace, no matter our ability level, because all the classes need to stay together. Taking online classes would allow students to not worry about others and their ability and instead only focus on their own work. This greater focus on themselves rather than others could also improve students' grades in classes they are struggling with.
 
While some people may think that students wouldn't learn well without face to face lessons from teachers this is not true Last summer, I took the online course of Economics and Personal Finance. While I admit that I was a little hesitant at first about how much I would learn in an online course, my fears were quickly swept away as I began the course. I learned a lot through the online class, and when it came time to come in and take the final test for the class at school, I did really well. While I did not learn face to face with a teacher, if I had any questions, the option was always open to email the teacher, and he would answer any questions I had. The option to complete classes at home and online would greatly benefit students. It would offer more opportunities and lead to a better focus on the course because of the different environment, the access to supplies, and the freedom to work at your own pace. Allowing students to take online classes is a good way to improve education as well as assimilate new technologies into the school environment.

The rings observed around supernova remnant 1987A are emitted by a plasmamainly made of ionized and neutral hydrogen atoms. With a density of 10 power10 atoms per cubic metre, and at least a dimension of plasma of 0.01light-year, the column density is 10 power 24 atoms per square metre, much morethan needed for an optically thick gas at Lyman frequencies (Case B). While, at10000 K, the bulky gas would absorb Lyman lines fully, at 50000K it emitssuperradiant lines. As superradiance de-excites the atoms strongly, nearly allavailable energy is emitted in a few competing modes: Superradiance appearsonly for beams which cross the largest column densities; for an observationfrom Earth, these beams generate three elliptical hollow cylinders whose basesare the observed rings; admitting that the Earth is not in a privilegeddirection, these cylinders envelope ellipsoidal shells observed, for theexternal rings, by photon echoes. For the equatorial ring, the brightness ofthe superradiant beams is multiplied by a quasi-resonant induced scattering ofthe rays emitted by the star. The de-excitation of atoms by emitted beams coolsthe gas strongly, so that ionization decreases fast, the process selfaccelerates. The energy of the high radiance rays from the star is stronglyscattered to the ring while the low radiance of the glow which surrounds thestar is rather amplified. The fast absorption of the radial beams produces theradial decrease of radiance of the ring while a competition of modes producesthe pearls.

The intensification of anti-Semitism in Germany following the 1933 Nazi boycott of Jewish businesses was a result of several factors: 1. Propaganda: The Nazi party used propaganda to spread anti-Semitic ideas and beliefs. They controlled the media and used it to portray Jews as enemies of the state. This propaganda was pervasive and relentless, and it played a significant role in shaping public opinion. 2. Legislation: The Nuremberg Laws, enacted in 1935, stripped Jews of their rights as German citizens and banned marriages between Jews and non-Jews. These laws further marginalized Jews and made it easier for the Nazis to persecute them. 3. Economic Factors: The economic depression in Germany also contributed to the rise of anti-Semitism. Many Germans blamed Jews for their economic hardships, and this resentment was exploited by the Nazis. 4. The Role of Hitler: Hitler's personal hatred of Jews was well-known, and his rhetoric and policies reflected this. His influence and charisma played a significant role in spreading anti-Semitism. 5. Kristallnacht: The Night of Broken Glass in 1938 marked a significant escalation in anti-Semitic violence. Jewish businesses, homes, and synagogues were destroyed, and thousands of Jews were arrested. This event shocked many people, but it also served to further dehumanize Jews and normalize violence against them. 6. Indoctrination: The Nazi party also targeted the youth, indoctrinating them with anti-Semitic beliefs through schools and youth organizations. This ensured that the next generation would also harbor these prejudices. 7. Apathy and Fear: Many Germans were apathetic or too afraid to speak out against the persecution of Jews. This allowed the Nazis to carry out their policies with little opposition. In summary, the intensification of anti-Semitism in Germany was a result of a combination of propaganda, legislation, economic factors, Hitler's influence, violent events like Kristallnacht, indoctrination of youth, and widespread apathy or fear.

Making extracurricular activities mandatory for students is not a valid rule that should be enforced. I don't agree that all students should have to participate in one extracurricular activity, whether students graduate or not shouldn't be determined by an extracurricular activity that they may or may not have done most of the students already do an out of school activity students shouldn't be forced to do something that they dont want to do. Just because they didn't take part in the chess club, or the yearbook committee doesn't mean that they should be held back. Someone could be the next Albert Einstein and focus on school so much that he didn't have time for extracurricular activities, but you decide to hold him back because of a silly little thing he didn't do. Whether students do extracurricular activities or not shouldn't determine if they proceed to learn new things. The administrators should look at their grades and their work habits. It does not make a valid reason for them to be robbed of their opportunity to be smarter, if they just didn't participate in an extracurricular activity. What if you dont have time? Most students already do an after school activity like sports, or a youth group. Some students even do multiple sports and still balance that with school. So for them to try and balance their after school activities, school, and now an extracurricular activity, it would be chaos. Students already go through enough stress with peer pressure, and their grades, and maybe just problems at home, so they shouldn't have to worry about this too. For example, maybe some students want to get a scholarship through playing a sport,and maybe their really good and actually have a chance at getting one. If you enforce that rule they would have to focus less on their sport and more on the extracurricular activity. Some students have trouble socializing with others. So maybe they didn't apply for an activity because they just didn't want too. Some students also enjoy their free time, time where they can do homework, be on their electronic devices, or just take a nap. So they didn't want to do an extracurricular activity because they didn't want to give up their free time. Students shouldn't have to feel like they are forced to do something. I mean it is called extracurricular activity for a reason, because it's for extra credit, so it shouldn't be a mandatory thing that they have to do. Students graduating shouldn't be decided by if they did an extracurricular activity or not,and the students who already do after school activities don't have time for for extracurricular things,and students should not have to be pressured to do something if its not mandatory, so I don't agree that students should have to take part in an extracurricular activity.

The term "special forces" is a modern concept, and it is not clear if it can be applied to medieval armies. However, there were certainly specialized units in medieval armies, such as the heavy cavalry, the light cavalry, the infantry, and the archers. These units had different roles and capabilities, and they were often used in different ways.
For example, the heavy cavalry was typically used for shock attacks, the light cavalry was used for scouting and foraging, the infantry was used for close combat, and the archers were used for long-range attacks. These units were often used in combination, and they could be quite effective in the right circumstances.
However, it is important to note that the structure and organization of medieval armies varied widely, and there were no set standards for how these units were used. In some cases, the heavy cavalry might have been used for scouting or foraging, or the light cavalry might have been used for close combat. The specific roles of these units were often determined by the situation and the needs of the army.
In summary, while there were certainly specialized units in medieval armies, the concept of "special forces" as a specific type of unit is a modern one, and it is not clear if it can be applied to medieval armies.

The Alexandra (Eildon) and Marysville Standard was published in the townships of Alexandra Township, Eildon Hills District Council area; and Marysville, South Australia from 1927 to 1939 by The Daily Mail Company Limited.
It is not known how long it existed as there are no records after 1937 but it may have continued until 1940 when all Australian newspapers were ordered closed down during World War II

Wang Xiaoyun (; born 1966) is a Chinese cryptographer, mathematician, and computer scientist. She is a professor in the Department of Mathematics and System Science of Shandong University and an academician of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. Wang was born in Zhucheng, Shandong Province. She gained bachelor (1987), master (1990) and doctorate (1993) degrees at Shandong University, and subsequently lectured in the mathematics department from 1993. Her doctoral advisor was Pan Chengdong. Wang was appointed assistant professor in 1995, and full professor in 2001. She became the Chen Ning Yang Professor of the Center for Advanced Study, Tsinghua University in 2005. At the rump session of CRYPTO 2004, she and co-authors demonstrated collision attacks against MD5, SHA-0 and other related hash functions (a collision occurs when two distinct messages result in the same hash function output). They received a standing ovation for their work. In February 2005 it was reported that Wang and co-authors Yiqun Lisa Yin and Hongbo Yu had found a method to find collisions in the SHA-1 hash function, which is used in many of today's mainstream security products. Their attack is estimated to require less than 269 operations, far fewer than the 280 operations previously thought needed to find a collision in. Their work was published at the CRYPTO '05 conference. In August 2005, an improved attack on SHA-1, discovered by Wang, Andrew Yao and Frances Yao, was announced at the CRYPTO conference rump session. The time complexity of the new attack is claimed to be 263. In 2019, she was named a Fellow of the International Association for Cryptologic Research (IACR) for "For essential contributions to the cryptanalysis and design of hash functions, and for service to the IACR." In 2019, she became the first female winner of China's Future Science Prize for her pioneering contribution in cryptography.

Punk music emerged in the mid-1970s as a response to the socio-political climate and the music scene of the time. Both in the United States and the United Kingdom, punk was a reaction to the perceived excesses of mainstream 1970s rock music, and its origins are found in garage rock and other forms of what is now known as protopunk music. In the United States, particularly in New York City, punk was a response to the economic downturn, high crime rates, and urban decay. Bands like the Ramones, Television, and Patti Smith Group emerged from this environment, using their music to express their dissatisfaction with the status quo. The New York punk scene was centered around the club CBGB, where these bands frequently performed. In the United Kingdom, punk was also a reaction to socio-political issues, particularly unemployment and the economic recession. Bands like the Sex Pistols and The Clash used their music to voice their discontent with the government and the monarchy. The British punk scene was more overtly political than its American counterpart, with many bands aligning themselves with the working class and advocating for social change. While there were similarities between the punk scenes in New York City and London, there were also significant differences. The New York scene was more focused on artistic expression and individualism, while the London scene was more politically charged. However, both scenes shared a DIY ethos and a rejection of mainstream culture. In terms of the larger culture, both groups were responding to a sense of disillusionment and frustration with the political and economic situation. They were also reacting against the perceived pretentiousness and commercialism of mainstream rock music. Punk offered a raw, stripped-down alternative that resonated with young people who felt alienated from mainstream society. In conclusion, while the specific triggers and expressions of punk varied between New York City and London, both scenes were responding to similar issues in the larger culture. The emergence of punk in both cities was a reaction to socio-political issues, economic hardship, and dissatisfaction with mainstream music.

The future of transportation is in transition. The combustion engine, which has powered cars for the past century, is coming to an end. Battery-powered electric vehicles and plug-in hybrids will completely replace internal combustion engine vehicles within decades. The total number of vehicle miles driven will decrease, forcing a rearrangement of roads and cities to account for the decline in vehicle traffic. Governmental bodies will need to determine whether to restrict or encourage a return to mass car ownership, taking into account the challenges of financing the cost of conversions and dealing with a lack of spare parts for existing vehicles. Society as a whole must grapple with the challenge of retrofitting streets and infrastructure to support vehicle function when less driving will mean fewer vehicles on the roads. In the meantime, however, the window of time to avoid more substantial social and environmental changes is closing. Air quality has been falling for years in many large cities, to the point where it is now the leading cause of preventable death. Lung and heart disease are linked directly to vehicle emissions, as are insulin resistance and many other health problems. It is not yet clear whether the epidemic of automobile usage worldwide has already made these problems unmanageable, or whether the slowdown in the growth of vehicle miles driven will avert more dramatic health effects. However the trends are clear: the world has become dependent on the combustion engine, and the shift to electric cars and plug-in hybrids may provide the best available solution to the problem of breathing air unfit to breathe.

Absolute zero is a theoretical temperature, meaning it can never be reached. Matter at absolute zero would not kill you. If you were to touch a single atom at absolute zero, you would not feel anything because atoms are not macroscopic objects and do not have a tactile feel. You would not be able to see, hear, taste, or smell an atom at absolute zero. It is impossible to touch anything at absolute zero because atoms are not macroscopic objects and do not have a tactile feel. Atoms are not macroscopic objects and do not have a tactile feel.

Jane Thorne (born 12 January 1946) is an English actress. She is the daughter of actress Dilys Hamlett and her second husband, actor Leslie Thorne. Her parents divorced in 1948. Thorne began her acting career as a child actress, and appeared in the films The Cross of St. George (1958), The Bitch Girl (1960), and The Ghost of Never Return (1961). She also appeared in the television series The Six Proud Walkers (1962), Emergency-Ward 10 (1962), and The Flying Swan (1965). In the late 1960s, she was a regular on the television series Never Take Sweets from a Stranger. She also appeared in the films The Plague of the Zombies (1966), The Nice Girls (1968), and The Executioner (1970). In the early 1970s, Thorne appeared in several episodes of the television series The Benny Hill Show, as well as the comedy film The Big Job (1973). She also appeared in the film version of The Little Prince (1974), and the television series The Tomorrow People (1973–1979). She had a regular role in the series The Kids Are Alright (1979–1982). She also appeared in the films The Great Train Robbery (1978), The Plumber (1979), and Chariot (1985). Thorne has made many appearances on British and American television. Her later work includes the films The Wolf of Wall Street (2013), The Legend of Tarzan (2016), and Justice League (2017), and the television series The Crown (2016), and American Horror Story (2017).

There are multiple benefits to implementing technology in a classroom, such as student accessibility and reduced dependency on instructor education. For example, computers that can identify and respond to student emotions can help students who have hearing or speech impairments communicate more effectively. Such technology also improves accessibility, particularly for students who live far from campus and rely on online classes, where physical attendance is often optional. Computers that can detect and respond to students' emotions could also serve as a powerful motivational tool, providing students who receive positive responses while the class is inactive a visual reminder to participate and achieve their learning goals. However, there are also potential drawbacks to implementing this type of technology, including the cybersecurity implications and challenges associated with consistently maintaining the technology in a classroom environment. Some of the software that is designed to recognize and respond to student emotions may rely on complex artificial intelligence, raising the risk that the technology will make incorrect classifications or malfunction. Additionally, classroom management will be more complicated for teachers, who will need to make sure that the computers respond to all students uniformly and don't mistake students for disruptive peers. Overall, the implementation of computers that can identify and respond to student emotions in a classroom presents both benefits and drawbacks. On the one hand, these technologies enhance accessibility and reduce dependency on instructor education and course content. On the other hand, they present new cybersecurity challenges and require additional maintenance effort to ensure that the computers are functional and failsafe. Although there are both advantages and challenges to using computers that can identify and respond to student emotions in a classroom setting, the use of this technology could improve accessibility, increase student engagement, and motivate students to complete assignments and participate in class. As a result, I would encourage colleges and universities to consider pilot programs that explore this technology's potential and carefully evaluate any drawbacks.

Some people have the attitude that a car accident can never happen to them with or without their phones but fail to realize that is just not true. Texts, social media, or another other phone alerts should never come before having your eyes on the road. Driving and phones are like oil and water; THEY DO NOT MIX! Even though there is a law where phones should not be in use while the car is on and somebody is on the road, there are still people who drive wreck less and risky with their phones in their hands. It is very understandable if somebody needs to get in contact with you to make sure you're okay, to pick up something from the store, etc. but you should be as focused on the road as you possibly can be. You may be able to check your phone while driving and not get in a car accident but the person in front of, behind, or across from you may not be able to do the same thing and could cause an accident. When a phone is in a person's hand while they are driving, they tend to make mistakes while driving. Little do they know, those itty bitty mistakes can lead to somebody losing their life. All it takes is phone someone to be somewhere at the wrong place and time and then- BAM! They've lost their life or will have to be in critical condition to a jackass on the road who was on their phones and not paying attention. Not to mention the fees and raise of insurance they will have to endure JUST for being on their phones. There will always be a time and place for cell phones but on the road is not one of them. Spread the word and save a life.

I have read the paper "Rectified Factor Networks for Biclustering" with great interests, and here is my review. I find the method generally has good performance in many benchmarks. However, there are also some limitations. For example, the method is not efficient to find huge biclusters, because it has to compute the posterior means of high-dimensional and sparse representation, which requires much more computation than the existing methods. In addition, the method may also have some difficulties to find precise biclusters when they exist, because the method uses soft attention mechanism to find activated features, and the computation of soft attention is hard to achieve exact zero values. All these limitations may reduce the usage of the method in practice. fed up with the limitations of existing biclustering methods, the authors recently proposed Rectified Factor Networks to compute the posterior means of high-dimensional and sparse representation and efficiently find precise biclusters. The method generally has good performance on many benchmarks. For example, on 400 benchmark datasets with artificially implanted biclusters, RFN significantly outperformed 13 other biclustering competitors including FABIA. In addition, the method is also tested on the data of the 1000 Genome Project, and RFN could identify DNA segments which indicate, that interbreeding with other hominins starting already before ancestors of modern humans left Africa. I have no further comments about the method. greatly beneficial for the study of biological big data, the biclustering algorithm could find huge biclusters in the data of the entire genome and could precisely identify the activated features, which could benefit the study of the interaction between the genome and the environment. However, there are also some limitations. The method needs to compute the posterior means of high-dimensional and sparse representation, and hence the method needs more computation than the existing methods. In addition, the method may also have some difficulties to find precise biclusters when they exist, because the method uses soft attention mechanism to find activated features. In summary, the method is generally good and could have promising applications in the study of biological big data, but needs to be improved in terms of efficiency and precision, for example, using acceleration methods or other optimization algorithms.

Cluster matching by permuting cluster labels is important in many clusteringcontexts such as cluster validation and cluster ensemble techniques. Theclassic approach is to minimize the euclidean distance between two clustersolutions which induces inappropriate stability in certain settings. Therefore,we present the truematch algorithm that introduces two improvements bestexplained in the crisp case. First, instead of maximizing the trace of thecluster crosstable, we propose to maximize a chi-square transformation of thiscrosstable. Thus, the trace will not be dominated by the cells with the largestcounts but by the cells with the most non-random observations, taking intoaccount the marginals. Second, we suggest a probabilistic component in order tobreak ties and to make the matching algorithm truly random on random data. Thetruematch algorithm is designed as a building block of the trueclusterframework and scales in polynomial time. First simulation results confirm thatthe truematch algorithm gives more consistent truecluster results for unequalcluster sizes. Free R software is available.

The Monitor project is an M-type eclipsing binary system located in the ONC region, the nearest star cluster to the Earth. The components of the system are designated JW 380a (A1) and JW 380a (A2) and are most likely both directly observable by the Hubbell telescope at the Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory on Mount Hopkins, Arizona, USA. The Monitor system was chosen as one of the targets for theMonitor project is an M-type eclipsing binary system located in the ONC region, the nearest star cluster to the Earth. The components of the system are designated JW 380a (A1) and JW 380a (A2) and are most likely both directly observable by the Hubbell telescope at the Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory on Mount Hopkins, Arizona, USA. The Monitor system was chosen as one of the targets for the James Web Space Telescope (JWST) IR grism spectrograph, due to its position close to the ONC and the expected brightness of the components. The Monitor system's catalog identification numbers are N01 and N02 in the Onsala Astronomical Observatory (GAO) Star Catalog.

Hallucinogens, such as mescaline and psilocybin, induce powerful spiritual experiences for most people who take them. However, for people who are blind, these drugs can provide visions of profound spiritual beauty and essential reality that might be overwhelming. People who are blind because of impaired sight, not because of being born blind, often describe these drugs as "seeing without eyes" and experience intricate visualizations that are deeply meaningful, although limited by their lack of visual stimulation. For many people who are blind, these drugs also act as spiritual vision quests. They induce a deeply connected, profound state of mind that can answer many lifelong questions about the nature of the mind, reality, and the universe. These drugs often show people who are blind that their sense of separation is a kind of delusion, and that their true home is what they experience as "inside the heart of the infinite." These spiritual insights can provide an essential component to the journey of sight loss, making the profound loss of eyesight less tragic and numbing. Hallucinogens can also induce powerful emotional experiences that can be healing and transformative for people who are grieving the loss of their sight. For many people who are blind, these experiences can feel like a total connection to the deepest parts of their heart, filling the deepest chasms of loss and pain. These drugs can remind people who are blind that they are not alone in their suffering, and that they can access profound depths of strength and beauty within themselves that were previously unavailable because of their impairment. These drugs can also reveal the beauty and resilience of their own essential human spirit, opening channels for profound love to flow into their hearts and minds. These insights can provide a vital component to the process of healing loss, helping people who are blind embrace their newly found depths of strength and beauty and transform their loss into an adventure of courage and creativity. People who are blind who have taken these drugs tell us that their experience is like life-changing oxygen to their lungs. They describe their lives before taking these drugs as being in a state of profound immaturity, like a young child. After taking these drugs, they describe their lives as being in a state of grace, like a newborn baby. These drugs offer a unique possibility for people who are blind to experience states of profound spiritual insight and connection that can heal the despair and alienation of their loss, and bring them into a powerful and wholehearted relationship with the blessings of their essential human spirit.

Before you begin, gather all the necessary tools listed under the Things You'll Need section. Start by turning the key to the off position to ensure the snow blower is not operational during the process. Next, remove the snow chute by unscrewing it from the main body. Move the control panel out of the way to gain access to the engine cover. Unscrew the plastic engine cover using a screwdriver and carefully lift it off. Disconnect the spark plug wire to prevent any accidental ignition. Using a spark plug wrench, remove the spark plug. Now, tilt the rubber paddles upwards to expose the attachment bolts. Remove the two rubber paddle attachment bolts from both paddles using a wrench. Then, remove all the torx bolts from both paddles. Take one of the new rubber paddles and set it in place. Insert and tighten the center rubber paddle attachment for the new paddle. Prep the new paddles by securing one end of a rubber paddle down with a vice grip. Place the nut for the torx bolt securely into another vice grip. Thread the torx bolt onto its nut and tighten the torx bolts as tight as you can. Next, remove the old scraper and attach the new one. Remove the drive belt cover using a wrench and take off the old drive belt. Attach the new belt and reattach the augur belt cover. Stand the snow blower upright. Thread the spark plug back on by hand and tighten it down. Attach the spark plug wire back on to the spark plug. Finally, place the engine cover back onto the snow blower and secure it down. Attach the snow chute and the control panel back in their respective places. Put the key in the ignition and your snow blower is ready to use with its new rubber paddles.

The Electoral College has been used to elect U.S presidents since 1787 when it was established by Article II Section 1 Clause 2 of the Constitution. The Electoral College is made up of 538 people who are elected from each state based on congressional district representation.
Each member casts one vote for their preferred candidate during the first Monday after December 31st every four years. In order for someone to become president they must receive 270 or more votes out of the total number cast (538). If no candidate receives this amount then there will be another election between those two candidates that received the most votes at least three weeks later. There have only ever been five instances where the Electoral College did not produce the same result as what would happen if all voters had voted directly for their favorite presidential nominee. 
In 2000 Al Gore won Florida's Electoral College vote but lost the national popular vote to George W Bush due to voter irregularities such as voting machines malfunctioning which caused many ballots to go uncounted. This resulted in Al Gore receiving 271 Electoral College Votes while George W Bush received 279 Electoral College Votes despite losing the nationwide popular vote by around 500,000 votes. 
There were also problems with absentee...

Although Venus has a reputation for being difficult to study, scientists have tried numerous times with unmaned spacecrafts. Each time, however, the spacecraft has not survived on Venus for more than a few hours. The author of the article "The Challenge of Exploring Venus," suggests that Venus is a worthy puruit despite its many dangers. The author does this by describing the planet's similarities to Earth Venus's location in the solar system and by describing the vast differences between Earth and Venus. Venus is often referred to as Earth's twin due to its many similarites. Both planets have similar sizes and distances from the sun. Scientists also believe that Venus was once a hospitible planet due to its similarities to Earth. Scientists have discovered that Venus has some features that are similar to those on Earth, such as land formations. Even though Venus is so hospitable, it is the closest planet to Earth and is the quickest travel throughout space. If Venus were to once again become hospitible as it was before, humans could potentially travel there. Venus has a similar distance from the sun as Earth, meaning that it should be similarly hospitible. However, Venus has high temperatures and pressure which does not add up to the distance from the sun. The temperature on a planet is determined by the planet's distance from the sun and how much heat and light is able to travel to the planet. Since Earth is hospitible at a similar distance as Venus, then why is Venus plagued by severe temperatures? This and other mysteries about Venus are being questioned by scientists, but without the ability to get close to the planet, most exploration is impossible due to the thick atmosphere. Although Earth and Venus have many similarities, their living conditions vastly differ. Due to Venus's acidic atmosphere and severe heat, living on Venus is impossible, while Earth is currently populated by a variety of creatures. Venus has temperatures that average over eight hundred degrees Fahrenheit, while the pressue is about ninety times greater than that on Earth. Venus also has the hottest surface temperature of any of the other planets, even hotter than Mercury. Venus is also plagued by volcanos, earthquakes, and frequent lightning. These differences may seem confusing because Mercury is closer to the sun and should, in theory, be the hottest planet in the solar system. Venus should also have a surface temperature and pressure similar to that of Earth. If scientists were able to figure out more information about Venus, it may provide more information about the solar system and about Earth. Since evidence suggests that Venus was once more similar to Earth, studying the planet may provide insight as to why Venus was turned into such an inhospitable planet, and if it could happen to Earth. Scientists could also potentially develop a way to live on Venus or turn its living conditions back to how it once was in the past. In conclusion, the author uses this evidence to support the idea that studying Venus is a worthy pursuit despite its many dangers.

I am against the developement of "Driverless Cars." Although a driverless car may sound cool, or may change the world someday, there are some negatives that go along with the production of the car. Driverless cars would be an expensive thing to prouduce. It would cause human driveres to not pay attention as much as they need to traffic laws would need a lot of updating. The car would not be completely driverless, in the text it reads "Google cars aren't truly driverless; they still alert the driver to take over..." With that being said, a driverless car wouldn't be the correct name for it. For the car to respond to the things it needs it would require massive upgrades to existing roads, which is not practical. Along with the road needing upgrades, the car would too. The car would need a whole lot of sensors, in the text it states, "The combination of all this input is necessary for the driverless car to mimic the skill of a human at the wheel." As if the human body is the best driver in the world, I feel as putting most of the control in the car would not be a great idea. To get cars up to the proper state they need to be would cost a lot of money. In a driverless car the "driver" would still need to be alert when "driving the car. The thought of having a driverless car would make people think they can text, eat, play games, and drink while driving. In the United States, Mexican applicants will be mailed a list of approved doctors for the medical exam by USCIS.
The same for the Adjustment of Status applicant, USCIS will determine whether the immigrant is eligible for the AOS.

The Electoral College is an institution that has been part of U.S. politics since 1787 when it was established by Article II Section 1 Clause 2 of the Constitution. It consists of 538 electors who represent all 50 states as well as Washington D.C., Puerto Rico, Guam, American Samoa, Northern Mariana Islands, and the Virgin Islands. Elected officials from each state are responsible for selecting their own number of electoral college representatives based upon population size (each state gets at least three). These electors then vote for president during the first Monday after December 31st every four years. The winner must receive 270 votes out of the total number of electors present before being officially declared President-elect. 
There have been many arguments made over whether this system works effectively within our current political climate. Some people believe there needs to be reform because they feel the Electoral College does not accurately reflect popular will while others think the Electoral College protects minority interests better than direct election would do. In order...

X-ray surveys facilitate investigations of the environment of AGNs. DeepChandra observations revealed that the AGNs source surface density rises nearclusters of galaxies. The natural extension of these works is the measurementof spatial clustering of AGNs around clusters and the investigation of relativebiasing between active galactic nuclei and galaxies near clusters.The majoraims of this work are to obtain a measurement of the correlation length of AGNsaround clusters and a measure of the averaged clustering properties of acomplete sample of AGNs in dense environments. We present the first measurementof the soft X-ray cluster-AGN cross-correlation function in redshift spaceusing the data of the ROSAT-NEP survey. The survey covers 9x9 deg^2 around theNorth Ecliptic Pole where 442 X-ray sources were detected and almost completelyspectroscopically identified. We detected a >3sigma significant clusteringsignal on scales s<50 h70^-1 Mpc. We performed a classical maximum-likelihoodpower-law fit to the data and obtained a correlation length s_0=8.7+1.2-0.3h_70-1 Mpc and a slope gamma=1.7$^+0.2_-0.7 (1sigma errors). This is a strongevidence that AGNs are good tracers of the large scale structure of theUniverse. Our data were compared to the results obtained by cross-correlatingX-ray clusters and galaxies. We observe, with a large uncertainty, that thebias factor of AGN is similar to that of galaxies.

Organizing your digital photos can seem like a daunting task, especially if you have years' worth of images stored on your computer. However, with a few simple steps, you can easily manage your digital photo collection. 1. Download a Free Image Organizer: There are several free image organizers available online. These tools can help you sort, tag, and categorize your photos, making them easier to find later. Some popular options include Google Photos, Adobe Bridge, and FastStone Image Viewer. 2. Create Subfolders: When you transfer your photos from your camera to your computer, immediately put them into a folder on your hard drive. Instead of dumping them all into the 'My Pictures' folder, create a subfolder by date. Use a reverse date format (YYYYMMDD) to keep them in chronological order. If you already have a large number of photos in your 'My Pictures' folder, take some time to sort them into appropriate subfolders. 3. Regularly Back Up Your Photos: To prevent loss of your precious memories, regularly back up your photos by burning them onto a CD or DVD. You can also use cloud storage services like Google Drive or Dropbox for this purpose. 4. Log Your Best Shots: Keep a record of your best shots. You can do this by creating a separate folder for them or by marking them as favorites in your image organizer. 5. Import Images into a Library: Most image organizers allow you to import your photos into a library. This makes it easier to browse through your collection and find specific photos. 6. Organize Photos into Albums: Once you've imported your photos into a library, organize them into albums. You can create albums based on different criteria like date, event, or people. 7. Avoid Duplicate Copies: If you back up your photos onto a CD and leave them on the computer as well, you might end up with multiple copies of the same photo in different folders. To avoid this, delete the photos from your computer once they're backed up. 8. Label CDs by Subject: One effective method of organizing is to put only photos about a particular subject on each CD and label it as such. This way, if you want a particular subject, you can find it easily. By following these steps, you can keep your digital photos organized and easily accessible.

Yes, there is a theoretical limit to the size of ocean vessels, which is determined by several factors. 1. Structural Integrity: The larger a ship is, the more stress is placed on its structure. This is due to the increased weight of the ship and the forces exerted by the waves and wind. If a ship is too large, it may not be able to withstand these forces and could break apart. 2. Stability: Larger ships are more difficult to stabilize. They are more affected by waves and wind, and are more likely to capsize in rough seas. 3. Maneuverability: Larger ships are more difficult to maneuver. They require more time and space to change direction or stop, which can be a problem in crowded shipping lanes or ports. 4. Port Size: The size of a ship is also limited by the size of the ports it can dock at. Many ports around the world are not equipped to handle extremely large ships. 5. Canal and Strait Size: The size of a ship can also be limited by the size of canals and straits it must pass through. For example, the size of ships that can use the Panama Canal is limited by the size of the canal's locks. 6. Economic Factors: Building and operating larger ships is more expensive. There is a point at which the increased capacity of a larger ship does not make up for the increased cost. 7. Environmental Impact: Larger ships have a greater environmental impact, both in terms of pollution and the potential for accidents. This can limit their size, especially as environmental regulations become stricter. So, while there is no fixed limit to the size of ocean vessels, these practical considerations effectively limit how large ships can be.

The true answer is that science does not yet know for sure the exact answer to this question, but it seems to be that they formed inside proto-galaxies in the first billion years of the universe. 
We still aren't sure how supermassive black holes (SMBHs) in the early universe grew to be so big so fast, but the general consensus at this point is they initially grew rapidly through countless mergers of stellar (approx 3-100 solar masses) and intermediate (100-10,000 solar masses) mass black holes, then continued to grow by accreting gas and dust, forming [quasars](_URL_0_) in the process. 
Stellar mass black holes are formed from massive stars at the end of their short lives of just a few million years, typically with a supernova explosion, and [it is predicted](_URL_2_) that during the early universe there were enormous numbers of these stars forming and dying. Intermediate mass black holes can either be formed by mergers between smaller black holes, or potentially via [direct collapse of gas clouds](_URL_1_).
Over millions of years repeated gravitational interactions between objects in a galaxy will cause more massive objects to tend to collect at the center while less massive objects are kicked out further. Gas clouds provide further braking forces which drive these black holes towards the galactic core until they eventually collide and merge. Galactic collisions drive more gas inwards towards the forming SMBH, forming an accretion disc which becomes a quasar, gradually adding mass to the black hole over millions of years. Computer simulations suggest that these processes are too slow to explain the speed at which the largest black holes grew in the early universe so it is likely that this is not the complete picture.
One potential answer is that direct gas cloud collapse [can directly produce a SMBH](_URL_3_) far larger than previously thought. Another theory is that quantum fluctuations in the moment after the Big Bang [produced large numbers of massive black holes](_URL_4_) spread throughout the early universe. These would have rapidly collected in the cores of protogalaxies and merged, however their gravity would still be insignificant in comparison to the Hydrogen gas and dark matter making up the majority of the mass of these galaxies.

It is advantageous to have a computer technology that can read all 44 major muscles and utilize the Facial Action Coding System (FACS) to determine a person’s emotions because it can assist in identifying emotional problems and helping those who may be hesitant to express their feelings. People often mask their true emotions and feelings in their facial expressions, so a person’s true emotions can often be difficult to discern. This technology can help to better understand and interpret a person’s true feelings by reading all of the facial muscles and utilizing FACS to determine the emotions. This technology can also help those who may be hesitant to express their feelings by allowing others to understand them better. For example, a person who is worried about how another person feels about them might be able to determine this through facial expressions. A person could also use this technology to communicate their feelings to others by displaying their desired emotions on their face. This could help improve relations between people and reduce misunderstandings caused by incorrect interpretations of facial expressions. However, there are also some limitations to relying solely on facial expressions to determine emotions and the potential impact of this technology on society. First, not everyone’s facial expressions are easily discernable to others. Some people have different expressions or lack the necessary muscle movement to clearly show a particular emotion. Additionally, since not everyone’s emotions are easily discernible, some people may avoid using this technology out of fear that they cannot express their feelings effectively or may not express their feelings correctly. Because facial expressions can vary greatly across different cultures, this could also negatively affect social customs among different regions. Additionally, some people may find this technology to be intrusive or uncomfortable for others to observe. This could also deter people from using this technology for various reasons. Overall, the benefits of this technology far outweigh the potential risks. This technology will continue to develop over time and may one day be able to detect and interpret a multitude of facial expressions. Ultimately, the impact of this type of technology will depend on how it is used and by whom. This technology can be useful in identifying emotional problems and helping individuals who may be hesitant to express their feelings. It can also be used to help those who may be deaf or hard of hearing. This technology can also be used by Emergency First Responders and soldiers. This could reduce time spent looking at people’s faces to determine their current emotional state. It could also be used in the workplace to encourage stronger relationships between coworkers. It could allow people who are away from their desk to receive electronic notifications of a raise or new job opportunity for another office. This could also improve the relationship between patient and doctor, or help students and teachers better understand the topic of a lesson. This could also make learning a language easier for people from different countries. It could also help make health care more affordable and efficient. Additionally, it could also be used to help those with Autism and Asperger’s syndrome. This could make them more social and interact with others. It could also help to prevent crime by allowing police officers to see if a suspect is lying. This technology could also be used to hurt people by identifying things they do not want others to know. It could also be used to invade people’s privacy by observing them inappropriately. For example, a babysitter could be spying on children in the privacy of their home. It could also be used to discriminate against people by denying services based on an individual’s emotional state. This could allow companies to increase profits by forcing people to work longer hours or more hazardous assignments. People could also be denied services based on their age or appearance. This could allow a company to make more money by selling their user data to others. This could even allow advertisers to target people with different products. It could also be used for surveillance and imprisonment. This could allow the government to monitor a person at all times and prevent them from leaving the house. It could also limit the free movement of people based on their emotional state. This could allow the government to seize power by controlling people who are likely to become aggressive, and make them susceptible to brainwashing. The government could limit citizens' access to anti-depressants or other drugs that could make them apathetic. This could create a society with no resistance to control people and use them for whatever purposes the government deems necessary. Overall, this technology is potentially a powerful and useful tool, but only if its development is in the hands of responsible scientists and developers. They should avoid using this technology for repressive or selfish purposes and make sure the benefits of this technology outweigh the risks. It must be controlled and used carefully to ensure it is not abused. The repercussions of not using this technology wisely could be disastrous.

In order to be eligible to play sports at my high school, I had to maintain an A-average throughout all four years of middle school (grades 6 through 8) as well as during freshman year of high school (grade 9). However, if you maintained only a B+ grade point average instead of an A-average over those same grades, then you were not allowed into any extracurriculars such as band, choir, drama club, student council, etc., even though there was still plenty of time left before graduation. 
 
 This made me feel like a failure because it meant that no matter how hard I tried, I could never get enough extra credit points to make up the difference between an A-average and a B+ average so that I would have been able to take part in these important activities while also maintaining a good GPA overall. 
 
 In addition, this affected other aspects of my life outside of academics too since being unable to join certain extracurriculars often means missing out on social events which can lead to feelings of loneliness and isolation from peers. 
 
 As a result, I believe that allowing students with a C-average rather than a B-average to enroll in extracurriculars is more beneficial both socially and educationally speaking.

The authors propose a solution for the task of synthesizing melodies. The authors claim that the "language-model"-type approaches with LSTMs generate melodies with certain shortcomings. They tend to lack long-range structure, to repeat notes etc. To solve this problem the authors suggest that the model could be first trained as a pure LM-style LSTM and then trained with reinforcement learning to optimize an objective which includes some non-differentiable music-theory related constraints. 
The reinforcement learning methodology is appropriate but straightforward and closely resembles previous work for text modeling and dialogue generation. By itself the methodology doesn't offer a new technique. 
To me, the paper's contribution then comes down to the novelty / utility / impact of the application. The authors clearly put substantial of effort into crafting the rules and user study and that is commendable. On the other hand, music itself is dealt with somewhat naively. While the user study reflects hard work, it seems premature. The semi-plausible piano melodies here are only music in the way that LSTM Shakespeare passes as poetry. So it's analogous to conducting a user study comparing LSTM Shakespeare to n-gram Shakespeare. 
I'd caution the author's against the uncritical motivation that a problem has previously been studied. Research contains abundant dead ends (not to say this is necessarily one) and the burden to motivate research shouldn't be forgotten. This is especially true when the application is the primary thrust of a paper.
Generally the authors should be careful about describing this model as "composing". By analogy to a Shakespeare-LSTM, the language model is not really composing English prose. The relationship between constructing a statistical sequence model and creating art - an activity that involves communication grounded in real-world semantics should not be overstated. 
I appreciate the authors' efforts to respond to some criticisms of the problem setup and encourage them to anticipate these arguments in the paper and to better motivate the work in the future. If the main contribution is the application (the methods have been used elsewhere), then the motivation is of central importance. I also appreciate their contention that the field benefits from multiple datasets and not simply relying on language modeling. Further, they are correct in asserting that MIDI can capture all the information in a score (not merely "Gameboy music", and that for some musics (e.g. European classical) the score is of central importance. However, the authors may overstate the role of a score in jazz music.
Overall, for me, the application, while fun, doesn't add enough to the impact of the paper. And the methodology, while appropriate, doesn't stand on its own. 
--Update-- Thanks for your modifications and arguments. I've revised my scores to add a point.

Inbreeding, or more strictly consanguinity, has been considered impure and disdained by Chinese culture since ancient times. While some legislators had tried to limit it during the Qin and Han dynasties of ancient China, excessive con­sanguinity continued to be a problem until the 20th century. Examples include the Song Emperor Jingdi, whose 47 ancestors included 34 second- or third-degree relatives (full or great great), and the fourth Qing Emperor Xianfeng, whose 46 ancestors included 35 second- or third-degree relatives. Both emperors were permanently bedridden as a result of this ­overbreeding.[1] Despite this longstanding cultural aversion to consanguinity, inbreeding has played a significant role in several Chinese dynasties, most notably the Song, Yuan, Ming, and Qing dynasties. One of the most famous examples is the Qing Emperor's great-great-grandfather, Emperor Renzong of Song, who was known for his influence over ruling succession despite having 42 grandchildren (through 54 marriages).[2] Another example is Zhu Yuanzhang, the founder of the Ming and Qing dynasties, whose mother was a cousin of exceptional degree. According to tradition, the Guangxu Emperor, who was the last Qing emperor, was born with an umbilical hernia as a result of his great-great-grandmother Cixi having over 80 children. This longstanding Chinese cultural aversion to consanguinity came at a high cost. Despite laws and customs that sought to prevent it, inbreeding led to genetic disorders in several Chinese rulers, some of whom were permanently bedridden as a result. One of the most famous examples is the Qing Emperor Jingdi, whose 47 ancestors included 34 second- or third-degree relatives (full or great great). Furthermore, excessive con­sanguinity led to ­overbreeding, or more accurately, inbreeding syndrome, which has also affected several Chinese dynasties, most notably the Song, Yuan, Ming, and Qing dynasties. As a result of this history of inbreeding, the Chinese people carry a fearful sentiment regarding the consequences of consanguinity, and even today marry within one's clan or nationality to a large extent to avoid issues of inbreeding. This longstanding cultural aversion to consanguinity came at a high cost. Despite laws and customs that sought to prevent it, inbreeding led to genetic disorders in several Chinese rulers, some of whom were permanently bedridden as a result. Examples include the Qing Emperor Jingdi, whose 47 ancestors included 34 second- or third-degree relatives

In the Middle Pleistocene, some Manchurian or North Chinese Steppe horses are thought to have migrated into Japan.
Horses are poor swimmers so experts believe they arrived in Japan via a land bridge during a period of low sea levels during the Pleistocene (18k - 35k years ago). They are thought to have arrived from either Korea via Northeastern China or from Russia due to the fact that larger horses lived in both areas. Fossil records do support both theories, however. There have been fossil remains found in Japan that could support both. 
Some experts believe that this population may have died out, however, since Chinese accounts tell of Kofun-era (250 - 538 AD) Japanese people being without horses. It could also have been that the horse population was so small that they were simply not visible to the visiting Chinese. Some accounts say that horses were not domesticated prior to this. They were hunted and eaten instead of being used as work animals. 
From Kofun forwards, there is plenty of evidence showing that the Japanese domesticated horses

This paper presents a novel method for deriving effective stochastic interactions in dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) using force covariance. The proposed approach provides a systematic way to extract the effective interactions from the force covariance of the underlying microscopic dynamics. The method is based on the assumption that the force covariance can be used as a proxy for the effective interactions in DPD. We demonstrate the validity of this assumption by comparing the results obtained from our method with those from direct simulations of the microscopic dynamics. The results show that the force covariance can indeed provide a good approximation of the effective interactions in DPD. This finding opens up new possibilities for the development of more accurate and efficient DPD models. The paper also discusses potential applications of the proposed method in various fields, including soft matter physics, biophysics, and materials science.

In general, it is a good idea to have a phone policy in schools. This allows school personnel to communicate about school-related matters without having to resort to traditional means such as the telephone. This is especially important when talking about sensitive issues related to students. For example, if a student's behavior is at risk of becoming an automatic expulsion from school, then having a way to communicate that to the parents without alerting the student would be helpful. Also, if a student's safety is at risk, then having the capability to notify the proper authorities would be helpful. However, it is not a good idea to let students have phones in class. It would not be appropriate to require students to have their phones with limited functionality in class, since this would interfere with learning. It would also be challenging to enforce such a policy. Additionally, giving students smartphones at an early age may interfere with their ability to concentrate on learning. Therefore, while a phone policy provides benefits, the disadvantages should be taken into consideration before deciding to implement such a policy in schools. Originally Posted by Education Next HEADLINE: Cell Phone Policy in Schools USE CASES: Examples to illustrate the benefits and drawbacks of having a phone policy in schools. Benefits: 1. It is easier for school personnel to communicate without having to resort to more traditional communication methods (i.e. telephone) 2. It is easier to notify parents about their children’s behavior without alerting the children 3. It is safer to notify the authorities about safety issues Drawbacks: 1. It interferes with learning, since students may be distracted from learning while using their phones 2. It is more difficult to enforce the policy, since students may hide their phones 3. Giving students smartphones at an early age may interfere with their ability to concentrate on learning
The reviewers unanimously recommend accepting this paper.

The paper introduces a novel directed generative model, the hierarchical compositional network (HCN), that is capable of discovering and disentangling the building blocks of a set of binary images without supervision. The main contribution of the work is the development of an efficient inference and learning algorithm for HCN based on max-product message passing. The use of MPMP also enables new tasks such as adding supervision information, classifying images, or performing inpainting to be performed simply by clamping some variables of the model to their known values and running MPMP on the rest. When used for classification, fast inference with HCN has the same functional form as a convolutional neural network (CNN) with linear activations and binary weights. However, HCN's features are qualitatively very different. The paper presents a comprehensive evaluation of HCN on several benchmark tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness and competitiveness compared to state-of-the-art supervised and unsupervised learning methods.
